target.title: feat(context-pruning): cache media to disk during pruning instead of stripping
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Context pruning removes image/media blocks and replaces them with `[image removed during context pruning]`, losing media permanently from the session
- Why it matters: Agents lose access to previously-shared images mid-conversation, degrading multi-turn visual reasoning
- What changed: When `cacheMedia: true` is configured, pruned media is saved to disk (`~/.openclaw/media/cache/`) and replaced with `[media cached: <path>]` markers that agents can read back on demand
- What did NOT change: Default behavior (`cacheMedia: false`) is unchanged — existing pruning logic preserved

### Bug fixes included (post-review)

| # | File | Bug | Fix |
|---|------|-----|-----|
| 1 | `pruner.ts` | soft-trim + hard-clear: `PRUNED_CONTEXT_IMAGE_MARKER` text was discarded when a message went through both phases, breaking `writePrunedMediaCaches` | Replaced `softTrimmedIndexes` Set with `softPhaseMediaCount` Map; hard-clear re-injects the right number of placeholder markers |
| 2 | `extension.ts` | multi-image hard-clear: `writePrunedMediaCaches` restarted the full `refs` loop for every content block, so block N got ref 0's cached path instead of ref N's | Changed to a single `refIdx` counter advancing sequentially across all blocks |
| 3 | `media-cache.ts` | TOCTOU race: `access()` then `writeFile()` with concurrent `Promise.allSettled` writes — a racing caller could observe a partially-written file | Write to `${hash}.pid.tmp` then `rename()` atomically; concurrent writers landing first are handled gracefully |
| 4 | `media-cache.ts` | MIME normalization: `mediaCacheKind` and `buildCachedMediaMarker` passed raw MIME to `mediaKindFromMime`, so `image/png; charset=binary` or `IMAGE/PNG` → `kind=document` → `images.ts` silently dropped the cached image | Both functions now call `normalizeMimeType()` before classification/embedding |
| 5 | `pruner.ts` | `extractCachedMediaMarkers` preserved entire text blocks (including soft-trim head/tail and trim notes) into hard-cleared output, defeating the clear | Now extracts only the `[media cached: ...]` marker strings via regex, not the surrounding block content |

target.title: feat(exec): expose knownLongFlags in safeBinProfiles config schema
target.body:
## Summary

Expose the `knownLongFlags` field in the `safeBinProfiles` config schema, allowing operators to declare long flags as "known" for GNU abbreviation resolution without misusing `allowedValueFlags` or `deniedFlags`.

Closes #56805

## Problem

The `knownLongFlags` field exists internally in `SafeBinProfile` and is used by `validateSafeBinArgv` for GNU long-option abbreviation resolution. However, it is not present in:

1. The Zod schema (`ToolExecSafeBinProfileSchema`) — `.strict()` causes an "Unrecognized key" crash
2. The `SafeBinProfileFixture` type — no way to pass it through config normalization

For empty profiles `{}`, `knownLongFlags` auto-derives as `[]`, causing **all** long flags to be treated as unknown and rejected. The only workaround is to list every possible long flag in `allowedValueFlags` or `deniedFlags`, which changes their semantics.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/config/zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts` | Add `knownLongFlags: z.array(z.string()).optional()` to `ToolExecSafeBinProfileSchema` |
| `src/infra/exec-safe-bin-policy-profiles.ts` | Add `knownLongFlags` to `SafeBinProfileFixture` type; update `normalizeSafeBinProfileFixture` and `compileSafeBinProfile` to normalize and merge with auto-derived flags |
| `src/config/normalize-exec-safe-bin.ts` | Add `knownLongFlags` to the type cast in normalization |
| `src/config/schema.base.generated.ts` | Regenerated via `generate-base-config-schema.ts` |
| `src/infra/exec-safe-bin-policy.test.ts` | 6 new tests for the config path |

## Design Decisions

- **Merge, don't replace**: User-provided `knownLongFlags` are merged with auto-derived flags from `allowedValueFlags ∪ deniedFlags`, ensuring flags in those lists are always "known" even if not explicitly listed
- **Reuses `normalizeFixtureFlags`**: Same normalization (trim, dedupe, sort) applied to `knownLongFlags` as to other flag arrays
- **Backwards-compatible**: Existing configs without `knownLongFlags` work identically via auto-derivation

## Usage

```json
{
  "tools": {
    "exec": {
      "safeBins": ["mycli"],
      "safeBinProfiles": {
        "mycli": {
          "knownLongFlags": ["--verbose", "--version", "--help"]
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

## Test Plan

- [x] All 96 tests in `exec-safe-bin-policy.test.ts` pass (90 existing + 6 new)
- [x] All 90 tests across related test files pass (`exec-approvals-safe-bins`, `exec-safe-bin-runtime-policy`, `io.compat`, `doctor/exec-safe-bins`, `doctor-config-flow.safe-bins`, `pi-tools.safe-bins`)
- [x] Generated schema test passes
- [x] Pre-commit hooks pass (oxlint, type checking, conflict markers)

target.title: Fail closed on ambiguous gateway agent errors
target.body:
## Summary
- fail closed for `openclaw agent` when the gateway error indicates the remote request may still be active
- keep embedded fallback for recoverable gateway errors like "gateway not connected"
- add focused tests for timeout/closed fail-closed behavior

## Why
In real ClawTeam/OpenClaw unattended runs, a gateway timeout could be followed by an immediate embedded fallback on the same session. If the gateway-side turn was still alive and holding the session write lock, the embedded retry would contend on the same `.jsonl.lock` and turn a real in-flight run into a misleading session-lock failure.

This change makes the CLI fail closed for ambiguous gateway errors instead of re-entering locally on the same session.

## Validation
- `pnpm -C /Users/zky/.openclaw/workspace/openclaw-src test src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts`
- real local CLI check against a bad gateway URL now returns an explicit "without embedded fallback" error instead of falling back to embedded

target.title: fix(telegram): respect topic-level groupPolicy overrides for /commands in forum topics (fixes #56699)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #56699 — native commands (`/reset`, `/new`, `/status`) in Telegram forum supergroups were being processed by all bots even when a topic had `groupPolicy: \"disabled\"` configured.

**Root cause:** `resolveTelegramCommandAuth` called `evaluateTelegramGroupPolicyAccess` with `useTopicAndGroupOverrides: false`. This caused the policy resolver to skip `topicConfig.groupPolicy` and `groupConfig.groupPolicy` overrides, falling back to the account-level policy. When a topic was configured with `groupPolicy: \"disabled\"`, that override was ignored for commands but respected for regular messages.

**Fix:** Change `useTopicAndGroupOverrides: false` → `useTopicAndGroupOverrides: true` so native commands use the same topic/group-level policy resolution that regular messages use (`bot-handlers.runtime.ts:586`).

## Test plan

- [x] All 7 existing `bot-native-commands.group-auth.test.ts` tests pass
- [x] All 76 Telegram extension test files pass (1113 tests)
- [x] TypeScript type-check passes
- [x] Manual: in a forum supergroup with `topics["N"].groupPolicy: "disabled"`, `/reset` in topic N should respond with "Telegram group commands are disabled." only for bots that have that topic disabled

target.title: fix(infra): tolerate chmod EPERM on K8s fsGroup-mounted PVC
target.body:
## Summary

On Kubernetes deployments with `fsGroup`-mounted PVCs, `chmod(dir, 0o700)` fails with EPERM because the state directory is owned by root (with group ownership set to the fsGroup GID). This crashes exec approvals, task registry, task-flow registry, and plugin-state initialization on startup.

This PR extracts a shared `tryChmodSync` helper that:
1. Tries `chmodSync(target, mode)` as before
2. On EPERM/EACCES/EROFS: verifies the process can actually access the path via `accessSync`
3. If accessible → tolerates the failure (the fsGroup grants adequate permissions via group bits)
4. If not accessible → rethrows the original error

## Root cause

```
EPERM: operation not permitted, chmod '/home/openclaw/.openclaw'
```

K8s `fsGroup` volumes are owned by `root:<fsGroup>` with setgid bit. The container process runs as a non-root UID in the fsGroup, so it has full access via group membership but cannot `chmodSync` a root-owned directory.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/infra/chmod.ts` | New `tryChmodSync` helper — chmod with EPERM/EACCES/EROFS tolerance |
| `src/infra/chmod.test.ts` | 9 tests covering all error codes and access verification paths |
| `src/infra/exec-approvals.ts` | Use `tryChmodSync` instead of raw `chmodSync` |
| `src/tasks/task-registry.store.sqlite.ts` | Use `tryChmodSync` |
| `src/tasks/task-flow-registry.store.sqlite.ts` | Use `tryChmodSync` |
| `src/plugin-state/plugin-state-store.sqlite.ts` | Use `tryChmodSync` |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/chmod.test.ts
 ✓ infra (9 tests) 38ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  9 passed (9)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `chmod(dir, 0o700)` throws EPERM on K8s fsGroup-mounted PVC, crashing exec approvals and state initialization.
- **Real environment tested**: OrbStack Kubernetes v1.33.9, Node.js v24.15.0, macOS arm64 host.

### Live K8s reproduction

Pod spec:
```yaml
securityContext:
  runAsUser: 1000
  runAsGroup: 1000
  fsGroup: 1000
volumes:
  - name: state
    emptyDir: {}  # simulates fsGroup PVC
```

Terminal output from `kubectl exec`:
```
=== K8s fsGroup EPERM Reproduction ===
Node: v24.15.0
User: uid=1000(node) gid=1000(node) groups=1000(node)

--- State directory (fsGroup mount) ---
drwxrwsrwx 1 root node  0 May 20 07:00 .openclaw

Owner: 0:1000
Mode: 0o2777

--- Main branch: chmod(dir, 0o700) ---
chmod FAILED: EPERM EPERM: operation not permitted, chmod '/home/openclaw/.openclaw'

--- PR fix: tryChmodSync(dir, 0o700) ---
chmod failed with EPERM - checking access...
accessSync(R_OK|W_OK|X_OK) PASSED ✅
→ tryChmodSync tolerates EPERM, gateway starts normally

--- Verify: can create state files ---
Write+Read: ✅ {"test":true}
Cleanup: ✅
```

**Before fix**: `chmodSync` throws EPERM → gateway crashes on startup.
**After fix**: `tryChmodSync` catches EPERM, verifies access via `accessSync(R_OK|W_OK|X_OK)`, tolerates the failure. State files can be read/written normally.

- **What was not tested**: Full OpenClaw gateway startup inside K8s (would require building and deploying a custom image). The chmod behavior is validated end-to-end in the K8s environment.

Closes #83619

target.title: TTS: enforce standalone directive lines parsing
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: TTS directives were previously parsed with regex-heavy matching, which made standalone-line requirements implicit and easy to misinterpret (especially for text blocks and mixed line endings).
- Why it matters: Ambiguous parsing can cause unexpected directive activation, inconsistent cleaned text behavior, and regression risk when input formatting varies (e.g., CRLF, extra blank lines, multiple text blocks).
- What changed:
  - Introduced internal line-by-line parsing helpers in `src/tts/directives.ts` with explicit states (`normal` / `in_tts_text_block`).
  - Made standalone directive rules explicit in behavior:
    - `[[tts:...]]` is recognized only when it occupies a full trimmed line.
    - `[[tts:text]]` and `[[/tts:text]]` are recognized only when each appears on its own line.
  - Preserved external API shape (`TtsDirectiveParseResult`) and compatibility of returned fields.
  - Added regression coverage in `src/plugins/contracts/tts.contract.test.ts` for:
    - CRLF (`\r\n`) directive parsing.
    - Multiple consecutive `[[tts:text]]...[[/tts:text]]` blocks (first block wins).
    - Stable blank-line preservation in `cleanedText`.
  - Updated tagged-mode auto-TTS test input to use standalone block form so tests reflect intended directive semantics.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary):
  - No change to public parse result type shape.
  - No change to provider override token semantics beyond standalone-line triggering requirements.
  - No changes to unrelated TTS synthesis transport/provider runtime behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #<fill-if-applicable>
- Related #<fill-if-applicable>
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: Directive recognition rules were embedded in broad regexes, so “standalone line only” behavior was implicit rather than explicit in parser control flow.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Contract coverage did not lock in CRLF behavior, multi-block precedence, and blank-line stability around directive stripping.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): Existing parser behavior relied on regex replacements for both directive lines and `tts:text` block extraction.
- Why this regressed now: As directive usage expanded, edge cases became more visible (line ending variations and block formatting differences).
- If unknown, what was ruled out: N/A.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/plugins/contracts/tts.contract.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - Standalone directive parsing with CRLF.
  - First-block-wins behavior for multiple `tts:text` blocks.
  - Stable whitespace retention in `cleanedText` after directive removal.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The contract suite exercises parser behavior through the same exported seams used by runtime TTS flow.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing parse directive tests cover baseline extraction and inline literal non-trigger behavior.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A (new tests were added).

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Inline literal directive strings (embedded inside normal sentence text) remain non-triggering.
- Directive activation is now strictly tied to standalone lines for `[[tts:...]]` and `[[tts:text]]` block markers.
- Behavior is consistent across LF and CRLF inputs.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[text with directive-like patterns]
  -> regex replace paths
  -> directive activation could be harder to reason about from control flow

After:
[text]
  -> line scanner (normal / in_tts_text_block)
  -> standalone line checks
  -> structured parse result
  -> deterministic cleanedText + overrides

target.title: feat: fire session_end hook on subagent completion and cron session end
target.body:
## Summary

Extends the `session_end` plugin hook to fire in two additional lifecycle cases that were previously missing.

### Problem

The `session_end` hook only fired on session reset/replace (one narrow path in `session.ts`). This made it impossible for plugins to react to task completion without polling — blocking use cases like dispatch queues, memory consolidation, and post-session cleanup.

Tracked in:
- Fixes #10142 (session:end event for workflow integration)
- Related to #31266 (session-memory hook wanting session:end)

### Changes

**`src/agents/subagent-spawn.ts`**
- Fires `session_end` alongside the existing `subagent_ended` hook when a subagent completes
- Guarded with `hookRunner?.hasHooks('session_end')` — zero overhead if no listeners
- Uses `buildSessionEndHookPayload` for consistent payload shape

**`src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts`**
- Fires `session_end` after the cron isolated agent session completes and session entry is persisted
- Same guard pattern

**`src/plugins/types.ts`**
- Updated `session_end` comment to document all three trigger cases: session reset/replace, subagent completion, cron completion

**`CHANGELOG.md`**
- Added entry under Unreleased

### Backward Compatibility

- `subagent_ended` hook continues to fire alongside `session_end` — no breaking change
- All new calls are opt-in via the existing `hasHooks` guard

### Motivation

This unblocks the [openclaw-dispatch](https://clawhub.com) plugin (currently in development) which needs to know when sessions complete to drain a task queue without heartbeat polling.

target.title: feat(cron): add agent-turn mode for failure alerts
target.body:
Closes #56521

## What

Adds a new `agent-turn` mode for cron job `failureAlert` that injects the error as a system event into the agent session instead of sending an outbound notification (Telegram/etc).

## Why

When cron jobs fail, the current `announce` mode sends a notification to the user's chat. But the agent (main session) is unaware of the failure — the human has to relay the error manually.

With `agent-turn` mode, the failure alert triggers the agent directly via `enqueueSystemEvent` + `requestHeartbeatNow`, allowing the agent to:
- Diagnose the error autonomously
- Attempt a fix if possible
- Report the outcome to the human

## How

- New `failureAlert.mode: 'agent-turn'` option
- In `emitFailureAlert`, when mode is `agent-turn`, bypasses `sendCronFailureAlert` and uses the existing `enqueueSystemEvent` + `requestHeartbeatNow` path
- CLI: `openclaw cron edit <id> --failure-alert-mode agent-turn`

## Usage

```bash
openclaw cron edit <job-id> --failure-alert-mode agent-turn
```

Or in config:
```json
{

    "after": 1
  }
}

target.title: feat: Add opt-in ACP parent completion notify for sessions_spawn
target.body:
## What changed

This adds an opt-in ACP parent update mode for `sessions_spawn` so ACP `mode:"run"` completions can return to the parent session like subagent completions, instead of only surfacing as parent-side system events.

Key behavior changes:

- adds `parentUpdates?: "system" | "notify"` for ACP spawns
- keeps the default behavior unchanged
- allows `parentUpdates:"notify"` for ACP `mode:"run"` even without `streamTo:"parent"`
- keeps `streamTo:"parent"` as the progress relay switch
- routes terminal completion/error through the existing subagent-style announce flow when `parentUpdates:"notify"` is enabled
- falls back to the existing system-event path if notify/announce is unavailable

## Why

Today, ACP `sessions_spawn` run completions only surface to the parent session as system events.

That lets the parent session observe that work finished internally, but the completion signal primarily rides the heartbeat/internal relay path instead of a normal completion delivery path. In practice, channel-facing completion notifications can be missing or incomplete, and final result details are often lost.

This change fixes that gap by adding an explicit, non-default completion routing mode for ACP run spawns.

## Design

This intentionally splits progress relay from terminal completion routing:

- `streamTo:"parent"` continues to control progress relay
- `parentUpdates:"notify"` controls terminal completion announce

That split keeps progress on the lightweight best-effort relay path while reusing the existing announce/completion machinery for final outcomes.

## Compatibility

- default behavior remains unchanged
- ACP / ACPX execution flow is unchanged
- only parent reporting / relay / completion routing is updated

## Validation

Passed:

- `corepack pnpm test -- src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.test.ts src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts`
- `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 ./node_modules/.bin/tsc -p tsconfig.json --noEmit --pretty false`

Note:

- `src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts` still has unrelated existing local failures:
  - `sessions_list filters kinds and includes messages` timeout
  - `sessions_send supports fire-and-forget and wait` expects `historyOnlyCalls` length 8 but gets 9

target.title: fix: bind Claude permission replies to session
target.body:
### Motivation
- Fix a security issue where Claude permission replies (`yes/no <id>`) could be spoofed from any session because pending requests were keyed only by a short `request_id`. 
- Ensure permission replies are only accepted from the same session that originated the permission request to prevent cross-session or untrusted-channel approvals. 
- Preserve existing behavior for legitimate requests while closing the approval spoofing vector. 

### Description
- Require `session_key` in the `notifications/claude/channel/permission_request` schema and propagate it through the MCP server; changed `src/mcp/channel-shared.ts` and `src/mcp/channel-server.ts`. 
- Store pending Claude permission requests with the originating `sessionKey` and only accept `yes/no <request_id>` replies when the inbound message's `sessionKey` matches; changed `src/mcp/channel-bridge.ts`. 
- Add and update tests to cover session-bound permission handling and regression scenarios, including rejecting attacker-session replies and accepting trusted-session replies; changed `src/mcp/channel-server.test.ts`. 

### Testing
- Ran the targeted unit tests with `pnpm test -- src/mcp/channel-server.test.ts`, which passed (`1 passed, 6 tests`). 
- Ran the repository verification gate with `pnpm check`, which completed successfully (lint/type checks passed).

target.title: fix(gateway): keep two-phase exec approvals pending when no approval …
target.body:
fix(gateway): keep two-phase exec approvals pending when no approval clients connected

## Summary

- Problem: Two-phase exec approval requests (used by agent tools) were immediately expired with `no-approval-route` when no approval clients were connected, same as single-phase (CLI) requests.
- Why it matters: Agent tool calls need to stay pending so that Control UI or webchat can connect with `operator.approvals` scope and resolve the approval before timeout. Expiring them immediately breaks the two-phase approval flow.
- What changed: Added a `!twoPhase` guard to the fast-fail condition in `exec-approval.ts` so only single-phase callers fail fast. Two-phase callers now remain pending until timeout.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Single-phase (CLI) callers still fail fast when no approver is available. No changes to approval forwarding, timeout logic, or the approval manager.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: The fast-fail condition `!hasExecApprovalClients && !forwarded` did not distinguish between single-phase and two-phase callers, expiring both immediately when no approval clients were connected.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test existed that verified two-phase approvals are kept pending when no approval clients are connected.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): The fast-fail path was originally written for CLI (single-phase) only; two-phase support was added later without updating this condition.
- Why this regressed now: Two-phase approval flow began being exercised in environments where approval clients connect asynchronously (Control UI, webchat).
- If unknown, what was ruled out: N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Two-phase approval requests must NOT be expired when no approver clients are connected; they must remain pending and receive an `accepted` response.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The unit test directly exercises the branch condition with fake timers and verifies that `expire` is not called immediately.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): The prior test only covered the single-phase fast-fail path.
- If no new test is added, why not: New tests are included in this patch.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Agent tool approval requests now stay pending (instead of immediately failing) when no approval client is connected, giving operators time to connect via Control UI or webchat.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[agent tool call] -> requestExecApproval(twoPhase=true) -> no clients -> expire("no-approval-route") -> DENIED

After:
[agent tool call] -> requestExecApproval(twoPhase=true) -> no clients -> respond("accepted") -> wait for timeout or approval
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux / macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22+
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Gateway exec approval
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Start gateway with no approval clients connected.
2. Trigger an agent tool call that sends a two-phase exec approval request.
3. Observe the approval request status.

### Expected

- Two-phase approval stays pending with `accepted` status until timeout or a client connects and resolves it.

### Actual

- Two-phase approval was immediately expired with `no-approval-route`.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Two-phase approval stays pending; single-phase still fast-fails.
- Edge cases checked: Timeout expiry still fires for two-phase after `timeoutMs`.
- What you did **not** verify: End-to-end with live Control UI / webchat.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Two-phase requests now wait the full timeout before expiring if no client ever connects.
  - Mitigation: This is the intended behavior; the timeout is already set by the caller and acts as the natural upper bound.

target.title: fix(control-ui): restore header logo and favicon display
target.body:
## Summary

After upgrading to 2026.3.24, the Control UI header logo (red lobster icon) and favicon are missing. The favicon.svg file exists and is accessible at `/favicon.svg`, but the UI doesn't display it.

## Root Cause

`agentLogoUrl()` returned a relative path `favicon.svg` (without leading slash) when `basePath` was empty, which broke resolution in the browser. Additionally, `renderAvatar()` skipped logo rendering entirely when `basePath` was falsy.

## Changes

- `agentLogoUrl()`: Always return `/favicon.svg` (absolute path) when basePath is empty, instead of relative `favicon.svg`
- `renderAvatar()`: Render the logo for assistant role regardless of basePath value
- Updated tests to match the new behavior

## Files Changed

- `ui/src/ui/views/agents-utils.ts` — fixed path generation
- `ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.ts` — removed basePath guard
- `ui/src/ui/views/agents-utils.test.ts` — updated test expectations
- `ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts` — updated test expectations

Fixes #55600

target.title: fix(gateway): add per-subsystem timeouts to shutdown close handler
target.body:
## Summary

- A single hung subsystem `stop()` call (e.g. Telegram polling stuck during network recovery) can consume the entire 25 s `SHUTDOWN_TIMEOUT_MS` budget, preventing remaining subsystems from cleaning up and forcing users to `kill -9` the gateway process.
- Wrap each async stop/close call in `createGatewayCloseHandler` with a 5 s per-subsystem `raceTimeout`. When a subsystem exceeds its budget the close handler logs a structured warning and moves on, so the remaining teardown steps still run within the outer force-exit window.
- Extract `raceTimeout` + `SUBSYSTEM_STOP_TIMEOUT_MS` to a standalone `shutdown-timeout.ts` (zero external deps, independently testable).
- Add optional `log` param to `createGatewayCloseHandler` for structured timeout warnings.

## Motivation

Observed in production: Telegram `stopChannel()` hung during a polling stall recovery. The 25 s `forceExitTimer` in `run-loop.ts` fired before WebSocket and HTTP server teardown could run. `kill` (SIGTERM) did not exit; `kill -9` (SIGKILL) was required. Related: #51100, #49910.

The outer `forceExitTimer` is a hard backstop, but it is a blunt instrument — it does not distinguish which subsystem is blocking. Per-subsystem timeouts are defense-in-depth: they cap individual operations so the overall shutdown budget is shared fairly.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/gateway/shutdown-timeout.ts` | New — `raceTimeout` helper + `SUBSYSTEM_STOP_TIMEOUT_MS` constant |
| `src/gateway/shutdown-timeout.test.ts` | New — 6 unit tests (fast resolve, hung promise, log output, timer cleanup, error propagation, constant value) |
| `src/gateway/server-close.ts` | Wrap all async stop/close calls in `raceTimeout`; add optional `log` param |

## Test plan

- [x] 6 unit tests for `raceTimeout` pass locally
- [ ] Existing `server-close.test.ts` unchanged (pre-existing `bundledChannelPlugins` infra issue affects all 175 gateway tests on `main` — not introduced by this PR)
- [ ] CI lint + type check pass

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.body:
## Summary
- accept both resolved and canonical path variants when validating local media roots
- add regression tests for macOS `/tmp` and `/private/tmp` symlinked path combinations

## Why
On macOS, local files written under `/tmp/...` may canonicalize to `/private/tmp/...`. The previous validation compared only one resolved form, which could falsely reject valid local media under an allowed root. That broke Telegram local MP4 sends in our runtime even though `/tmp/openclaw` was already allowed.

## Impact
This keeps the existing local root boundary in place, but avoids false negatives when the media path and the configured root are the same location expressed through different symlink forms.

## Validation
- targeted Vitest run for `src/media/local-media-access.test.ts`
- targeted media tests on the same runtime patch set
- live smoke test sending a local MP4 from `/tmp/openclaw/...` through Telegram after rebuilding the runtime

target.title: fix(gateway): preserve indentation when recombining non-streaming replies
target.body:
AI-assisted: yes (OpenClaw agent-authored).
Testing: targeted local validation (gateway regression tests + targeted lint + pre-commit repo checks).

## Summary

- Companion to merged #55960, not a duplicate: #55960 fixed indentation loss while stripping inline reply directives in `src/utils/directive-tags.ts`; this PR fixes the remaining gateway-layer loss when non-streaming reply parts are recombined in `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts`.
- Preserve each non-streaming payload chunk as authored when recombining it, so indentation-sensitive content survives payload boundaries.
- Reuse that same combiner for `/btw` side results so sibling non-streaming gateway paths behave consistently.
- Normalize `CRLF` / `CR` / `LF` boundary trimming and add focused regression coverage for the single-newline, `/btw`, and CRLF cases.
- Scope boundary: no streaming-path formatting changes, no Markdown parsing changes, and no channel-specific formatting rules.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #42576
- Related #29377
- Companion to merged #55960

## Relationship to #55960

Merged PR #55960 fixed one real whitespace-loss layer: inline reply-directive stripping in the shared parser (`src/utils/directive-tags.ts`).

This PR fixes a separate remaining layer later in the pipeline: gateway non-streaming recombination of final reply chunks (and `/btw` side-result chunks) in `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts`.

So the two PRs are adjacent and complementary, not overlapping duplicates.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Non-streaming final replies and `/btw` side results now preserve indentation when formatted content spans multiple payloads. When one chunk already ends with a single newline and the next chunk does not start with one, the gateway now preserves that authored single newline instead of forcing a blank line. The combiner also trims `CRLF` / `CR` / `LF` cleanly at outer boundaries so Windows-style line endings do not leak stray carriage returns.

This fixes the flattened-output symptom reported against Matrix, but the bug itself is generic to gateway non-streaming recombination of indentation-sensitive content rather than Matrix-specific formatting.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local Node.js checkout
- Model/provider: n/a (gateway-side reply recombination)
- Integration/channel (if any): reproduced against the gateway non-streaming `chat.send` path; user report was on Matrix
- Relevant config (redacted): n/a

### Steps

1. Produce a non-streaming assistant reply whose formatted block is emitted across multiple final payloads.
2. Include indentation-sensitive content such as nested YAML, a nested list, or a fenced code block.
3. Observe the stored/broadcast final message text.

### Expected

- Indentation from each payload chunk is preserved in the final combined reply.

### Actual

- Leading whitespace at payload boundaries was trimmed away, flattening structured content.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Added focused regression coverage for:
- the authored single-newline separator case
- an indentation-sensitive `/btw` YAML side result
- `CRLF` boundary trimming

The linked user issue includes before/after screenshots of the real-world symptom.

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: targeted gateway regression test for the single-newline recombination edge case; targeted `/btw` side-result regression test for an indented YAML block; targeted `CRLF` boundary regression coverage; targeted lint on the changed files; repo pre-commit checks during commit.
- Edge cases checked: whitespace-only payload chunks are still ignored; separator insertion still only happens when adjacent chunks are not already separated by line breaks; authored single-newline separators are preserved instead of expanded to blank lines; `CRLF` boundaries do not leave stray carriage returns.
- What you did **not** verify: live Matrix delivery against a running instance, broader streaming reply behavior outside the existing `/btw` block-side-result coverage, or full-repo test suites.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert commits `6b2461a11`, `c753cd0af`, and `d667f18f1` (or revert the PR branch changes as a unit)
- Files/config to restore: `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts`, `src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts`, and `src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: unexpected extra blank lines between merged payload chunks, unexpected loss of an authored single newline between chunks, stray carriage returns from `CRLF` boundaries, or lost indentation in `/btw` side results

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: preserving authored separators changes the old trim/join behavior for payloads that already end in a single newline.
  - Mitigation: this is intentional, explicitly regression-tested, and still bounded by the same whitespace-only-chunk skipping plus separator insertion only when neither side already provides a line break.
- Risk: preserving chunk text could expose `CRLF` edge cases at outer boundaries.
  - Mitigation: the combiner now trims `CRLF` / `CR` / `LF` consistently at the start/end, with targeted regression coverage.
- Risk: reviewers may mistake this PR for a duplicate of #55960.
  - Mitigation: this description now names the exact layer split: `#55960` fixes directive parsing, this PR fixes gateway non-streaming recombination.

## Summary

target.title: fix(agents/harness): validate forced plugin harness support before pinning
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `selectAgentHarnessDecision()` returned the forced plugin harness for a candidate without consulting that harness's own `supports({ provider, modelId })` predicate. Once an agent's runtime was pinned to a plugin harness (e.g. `agentRuntime.id: "codex"`), every fallback-chain candidate flowed through that harness regardless of whether the harness could actually serve the candidate's provider.
- **Why it matters:** For Codex, this surfaced in production as a late `CodexAppServerRpcError: failed to load configuration: Model provider X not found` whenever an operator's fallback chain referenced an OpenClaw-only provider that was absent from `~/.codex/config.toml`. Observed on operator installs that have a local proxy provider (`9router`) configured under OpenClaw `models.providers` but not registered in Codex's own `[model_providers.*]` config. Combined with the long startup hang fixed in #74335, this turned a single cross-config gap into recurring overnight outages where every agent's tool calls dropped to zero.
- **What changed:** In the forced-plugin branch of `selectAgentHarnessDecision()`, call `forced.supports({ provider, modelId, requestedRuntime })` before pinning. If unsupported, route to PI when fallback policy permits, otherwise throw an unsupported-harness error including the candidate `provider/modelId` and the harness's `reason`. Extend Codex harness `DEFAULT_CODEX_HARNESS_PROVIDER_IDS` from `{"codex"}` to `{"codex", "openai", "openai-codex"}` to match the documented Codex routing ids (`docs/concepts/agent-runtimes.md`, `docs/concepts/model-providers.md`, `docs/gateway/config-agents.md`, `docs/plugins/codex-harness.md`) and the existing `resolveCodexAppServerModelProvider()` mappings in `extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.ts`.
- **What did NOT change:** The auto-mode harness selection (`runtime === "auto"`) was already calling `supports()` correctly. Session pinning precedence (`agentHarnessId` over env / config) is preserved. The behavior of `runWithModelFallback()` candidate enumeration is unchanged. Codex app-server protocol is untouched.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #74335 (this repo, the same operator outage's symptom-amplification fix bounding `withCodexStartupTimeout`)
- Related #42715 (Multi-provider AI Gateway: missing apiKey causes silent model fallback) — adjacent class of cross-provider fallback misroute
- Related #48680 (Model fallback treats HTTP 403 business rejection as `candidate_succeeded`) — adjacent class of fallback-decision correctness
- Related #64902 (Failed primary model attempt not logged when fallback succeeds — no forensic audit trail) — orthogonal observability gap
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- **Root cause:** `src/agents/harness/selection.ts` forced-plugin branch returned the harness whose `id === runtime` without consulting its `supports()` predicate. The harness's own opinion about which providers it can serve was bypassed entirely whenever the runtime was pinned, so cross-config fallbacks reached Codex's app-server, which then rejected them with `failed to load configuration: Model provider X not found` after sending the request through `extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.ts:97-101`.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** `harness.supports()` exists on every `AgentHarness` and is consulted in the auto-mode branch (`src/agents/harness/selection.ts:134-141`), but the forced branch trusted the runtime id alone. There was no contract test forcing every harness selection path through `supports()`.
- **Contributing context:** Codex's `DEFAULT_CODEX_HARNESS_PROVIDER_IDS` set was historically `{"codex"}` only, even though the documented native path is `openai/gpt-*`. The forced-branch bypass made the narrow set invisible. Tightening the selector (this PR) requires the set to match documented behavior, hence the `openai`/`openai-codex` additions.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- **Coverage level that should have caught this:** Unit test asserting the forced-plugin selection branch consults `supports()` for the candidate `provider/modelId`, plus a contract test for the Codex harness's accepted provider ids.
- **New tests added:**
  - `src/agents/harness/selection.test.ts`: (1) forced plugin harness with unsupported candidate provider falls back to PI when permitted, (2) raises clear error when fallback is `none`, (3) session-pinned plugin harness rejects unsupported candidate provider, (4) compaction routing for a plugin-pinned session whose provider the plugin does not support fails closed.
  - `src/agents/harness/selection.test.ts`: 2 pre-existing pin-precedence tests updated to use a Codex-supported provider so they continue to test their original intent.
  - `extensions/codex/harness.test.ts` (new file): Codex harness `supports()` accepts `codex`, `openai`, `openai-codex` (case-insensitive) and rejects foreign provider ids such as `9router`.

## Architecture / Flow Diagram

```text
Before:
selectAgentHarnessDecision({ provider: "9router", agentRuntime.id: "codex" })
  └─ forced = registered codex harness
  └─ return { harness: codex, ... }              ← supports() never consulted
        └─ Codex app-server: "Model provider 9router not found"   (late RPC error)

After:
selectAgentHarnessDecision({ provider: "9router", agentRuntime.id: "codex" })
  └─ forced = registered codex harness
  └─ forced.supports({ provider: "9router", modelId, requestedRuntime: "codex" })
        ├─ supported: true   → return forced harness          (when codex actually owns it)
        └─ supported: false
              ├─ policy.fallback === "pi"   → return PI harness, log warn
              └─ policy.fallback === "none" → throw "Requested agent harness 'codex' does not support 9router/cc/claude-opus-4-6 (provider is not one of: ...) and PI fallback is disabled."
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 25.3.0 (darwin)
- Runtime/container: npm-global install of `openclaw@2026.4.26`
- Model/provider: `openai-codex/gpt-5.5` primary; `9router` configured as an OpenClaw provider but not in `~/.codex/config.toml`
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A — gateway-internal failure
- Relevant config (redacted): `agents.defaults.model.primary = "openai-codex/gpt-5.5"`; agents pinned to `agentRuntime.id: "codex"`; `models.providers["9router"]` defined under OpenClaw

### Steps

1. Configure an OpenClaw agent with `agentRuntime.id: "codex"` and a fallback chain that synthesizes a `9router/cc/claude-opus-4-6` candidate (via `models.providers["9router"]`).
2. Provoke a primary-model failure (e.g. transient codex startup hang per #73520; or simply force codex offline for a turn).
3. Observe `gateway.err.log`: fallback chain advances `openai-codex/gpt-5.5 → openai/gpt-5.5 → 9router/cc/claude-opus-4-6`; the last candidate fails with `CodexAppServerRpcError: failed to load configuration: Model provider 9router not found` and `next=none`.

### Expected

- The fallback chain skips a candidate the forced harness cannot serve, either routing it to the embedded PI backend (when `policy.fallback === "pi"`) or surfacing a clear unsupported-harness error (when `policy.fallback === "none"`).

### Actual (before this PR)

- Codex receives the unsupported provider and produces the `Model provider X not found` error after the request has already been sent into the harness, with `next=none` ending the chain.

## Evidence

- Failing trace before (extracted from operator's `gateway.err.log`):
  ```text
  2026-04-29T05:40:44.788-05:00 [model-fallback/decision] decision=candidate_failed requested=openai-codex/gpt-5.5 candidate=openai/gpt-5.5 reason=timeout next=9router/cc/claude-opus-4-6
  2026-04-29T05:40:44.792-05:00 [model-fallback/decision] decision=candidate_failed requested=openai-codex/gpt-5.5 candidate=9router/cc/claude-opus-4-6 reason=timeout next=none
  2026-04-29T06:11:15.896-05:00 [diagnostic] lane task error: lane=main durationMs=5082 error="CodexAppServerRpcError: failed to load configuration: Model provider `9router` not found"
  ```
- Passing tests after:
  - `pnpm test src/agents/harness/selection.test.ts` → 26/26 pass.
  - `pnpm test extensions/codex/harness.test.ts` → 4/4 pass.
- Verified locally:
  - `pnpm tsgo:core` clean.
  - `pnpm tsgo:extensions` clean.
  - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1` clean on all 5 modified files.

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:**
  - Forced Codex harness with `provider: "openai"` / `modelId: "gpt-5.4"` continues to be pinned (documented canonical path; covered by new codex harness test and unaffected pin-precedence test).
  - Forced Codex harness with `provider: "9router"` and `policy.fallback === "pi"` routes to PI with a warn log; `selectedReason: "forced_plugin_fallback_to_pi"`.
  - Forced Codex harness with `provider: "9router"` and `policy.fallback === "none"` throws `Requested agent harness "codex" does not support 9router/cc/claude-opus-4-6 (provider is not one of: codex, openai, openai-codex) and PI fallback is disabled.`
  - Session-pinned (`agentHarnessId: "codex"`) with unsupported provider throws (covered by new test).
  - Compaction (`maybeCompactAgentHarnessSession`) routes the unsupported pinned-session candidate to the same throw path (covered by new test).
- **Edge cases checked:**
  - Auto-mode selection (`agentRuntime.id: "auto"`) is unchanged; existing tests pass.
  - PI runtime forced (`agentRuntime.id: "pi"`) unchanged.
  - CLI runtime aliases unchanged.
  - `agentHarnessId` (session pin) precedence over `agentRuntime` defaults preserved.
- **What I did NOT verify:**
  - Did not run `pnpm check` (full prod sweep) or `pnpm test` (full suite) locally; relied on targeted test files plus core+extensions typecheck and the changed-files gate. CI should run the broader gates.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` for any deployment where the forced plugin harness already supported the candidate provider via its `supports()` predicate (the documented Codex paths `openai/*`, `openai-codex/*`, `codex/*` continue to pin Codex). Behavior changes only for cross-provider candidates the harness could not have served correctly anyway: those now either route to PI (when permitted) or surface a clear unsupported-harness error instead of producing a late `Model provider X not found` from the underlying harness.
- Config/env changes? `No`.
- Migration needed? `No`.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** Some installs may have relied on the forced-pin bypass to route `azure-openai/*` (or another non-canonical id) through Codex's app-server.
  - **Mitigation:** Codex harness can be extended easily — add the additional id to `DEFAULT_CODEX_HARNESS_PROVIDER_IDS` or accept an explicit `providerIds` override on `createCodexAppServerAgentHarness({ providerIds: [...] })`. The current set covers the documented canonical paths; additional ids should be added with explicit owner-extension review.
- **Risk:** The new error message on `policy.fallback === "none"` is surfaced through synchronous selection rather than via a model-fallback decision row.
  - **Mitigation:** The error message is structured (includes provider/modelId/harness id and reason), so log/observation code can match on it. The previous failure mode produced a late `CodexAppServerRpcError` from a different layer, which was harder to attribute to the harness selection decision.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Forced plugin harness selection now consults the harness `supports()` predicate before pinning, so unsupported fallback providers do not get sent into Codex app-server and fail late as "Model provider X not found".
- **Real environment tested:** macOS operator OpenClaw gateway setup, npm-global OpenClaw install, agent pinned to Codex runtime with an OpenClaw-only `9router` fallback provider absent from Codex config. Evidence is from the operator gateway/runtime path described above, with secrets redacted.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran the PR branch harness-selection runtime checks and inspected the real gateway failure path/log excerpt that motivated the patch. Commands used: `pnpm test src/agents/harness/selection.test.ts`; `pnpm test extensions/codex/harness.test.ts`; `pnpm tsgo:core`; `pnpm tsgo:extensions`; `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1`.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal capture / copied live output from the operator setup and PR branch checks:

  ```text
  gateway.err.log before patch (real operator setup):
  2026-04-29T05:40:44.788-05:00 [model-fallback/decision] decision=candidate_failed requested=openai-codex/gpt-5.5 candidate=openai/gpt-5.5 reason=timeout next=9router/cc/claude-opus-4-6
  2026-04-29T05:40:44.792-05:00 [model-fallback/decision] decision=candidate_failed requested=openai-codex/gpt-5.5 candidate=9router/cc/claude-opus-4-6 reason=timeout next=none
  2026-04-29T06:11:15.896-05:00 [diagnostic] lane task error: lane=main durationMs=5082 error="CodexAppServerRpcError: failed to load configuration: Model provider `9router` not found"

  after patch, targeted runtime checks:
  pnpm test src/agents/harness/selection.test.ts -> 26/26 pass
  pnpm test extensions/codex/harness.test.ts -> 4/4 pass
  pnpm tsgo:core -> clean
  pnpm tsgo:extensions -> clean
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** The patched branch routes unsupported forced-Codex candidates to PI when fallback is permitted, or throws a clear unsupported-harness error when fallback is disabled, instead of invoking Codex app-server with a provider it cannot load.
- **What was not tested:** Full `pnpm check` / full repository test sweep was not run locally; broader CI should still run before landing.

target.title: feat: tool policy audit logging with rule attribution
target.body:
## Summary

Adds structured INFO-level log entries when tool policy decisions remove tools from the available set, answering the question: **"which rule blocked which tool?"**

Currently, when a tool call fails because a policy denied it, operators have no way to determine which configuration layer made the decision. This PR adds audit logging at two critical points.

## Changes

### 1. Pipeline filtering audit (`tool-policy-pipeline.ts`)

After each pipeline step filters tools, logs which tools were removed with the step's config label:

```
tool-policy: tools.deny removed 1 tool(s): browser
tool-policy: agents.mybot.tools.allow removed 2 tool(s): gateway, write
tool-policy: group tools.allow removed 3 tool(s): cron, exec, elevated
```

The 7-step pipeline already has labeled steps (`tools.profile`, `tools.allow`, `tools.byProvider.allow`, `agents.<id>.tools.allow`, etc.), so the audit log identifies the exact config key to fix.

### 2. Sandbox tool policy audit (`sandbox/runtime-status.ts`)

When the sandbox tool policy blocks a tool call at runtime, logs the decision with full provenance:

```
tool-policy: sandbox deny blocked "exec" (source=agent, key=agents.mybot.sandbox.tools.deny, mode=all)
tool-policy: sandbox allow blocked "browser" (source=global, key=sandbox.tools.allow, mode=non-main)
```

### Tests

- 3 new tests covering deny-list removal, allow-list removal, and no-removal cases
- All existing pipeline tests (8) and sandbox policy tests (12) pass

## Motivation

Closes #55801. Fleet operators running multi-agent deployments need to quickly diagnose why a tool is unavailable. The current behavior silently filters tools with no trace of which config layer made the decision. This is especially painful when policies cascade across 7 layers (profile → global → provider → agent → group).

## Test plan

- [x] New: audit log emitted when deny list removes tools
- [x] New: audit log lists all removed tools sorted alphabetically
- [x] New: no audit log when no tools are removed
- [x] Existing pipeline tests pass (8 tests)
- [x] Existing sandbox policy tests pass (12 tests)
- [x] All pre-commit checks pass

target.title: Add "verbose limit" support for configuring exec tool display truncat…
target.body:
● Summary

  - Problem: Verbose mode always truncated exec tool command display at a hard-coded 120 chars with no way to change it
  per session.
  - Why it matters: Users running long commands (e.g. complex shell pipelines) couldn't see enough of the command in
  tool logs to understand what was executed.
  - What changed: Added /verbose limit <n> command across TUI, web UI, and messaging (Telegram) surfaces. The limit is
  stored in the session entry (verboseLimit), persisted via sessions.patch, and threaded to compactRawCommand() through
  resolveToolDisplay → resolveExecDetail. A single DEFAULT_VERBOSE_LIMIT = 120 constant replaces the previous inline
  magic number.
  - What did NOT change: Default truncation behavior (120 chars) is unchanged when verboseLimit is not set. Verbose
  on/off/full logic is untouched.

  Change Type

  - Feature
  - Refactor required for the fix

  Scope

  - Gateway / orchestration
  - Skills / tool execution
  - API / contracts
  - UI / DX

  Linked Issue/PR

  N/A

  Root Cause / Regression History

  N/A

  Regression Test Plan

  N/A — new feature, no prior behavior to regress.

  - If no new test is added, why not: the feature is a display-only formatting parameter with no side effects on agent
  behavior or data integrity.

  User-visible / Behavior Changes

  - New command: /verbose limit <n> (integer ≥ 1) — sets max display length for exec tool command logs in verbose mode.
  - /verbose limit (no arg) — shows the current limit.
  - Default remains 120 chars when unset.
  - Setting persists across sessions (stored in session entry).

  Diagram

  Before:
  /verbose on → exec tool log always truncated at 120 chars

  After:
  /verbose limit 300 → stored in session
  exec tool log      → compactRawCommand(cmd, 300) → truncated at 300 chars

  Security Impact

  - New permissions/capabilities? No
  - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
  - New/changed network calls? No — uses existing sessions.patch RPC
  - Command/tool execution surface changed? No — display-only
  - Data access scope changed? No

  Repro + Verification

  Steps

  1. Start a session with /verbose on
  2. Run a long exec command — confirm truncation at 120 chars (default)
  3. Send /verbose limit 300
  4. Run the same long exec command — confirm truncation at 300 chars
  5. Restart/reload session — confirm limit persists

  Expected

  - Step 4: command display extended to 300 chars
  - Step 5: verboseLimit: 300 survives session reload

  Human Verification

  - Verified via Telegram: /verbose limit 1000 correctly stored and applied to exec tool display
  - Verified directive parser now captures numeric suffix (/verbose limit 50 → rawVerboseLevel = "limit 50")
  - Web UI and TUI paths verified in prior conversation turns
  - Not verified: cron-isolated-agent path (uses same AgentRunContext registry, should work)

  Compatibility / Migration

  - Backward compatible? Yes
  - Config/env changes? No
  - Migration needed? No

  Risks and Mitigations

  - Risk: verboseLimit not populated in AgentRunContext for runs registered before the session entry is resolved (e.g.
  gateway-side ACP runs).
    - Mitigation: The field is optional; missing it falls back to DEFAULT_VERBOSE_LIMIT = 120, preserving prior
  behavior.

target.title: feat(acp): forward image blocks in tool call results
target.body:
## Summary

- `extractToolCallContent()` in the ACP event mapper previously only extracted `type: "text"` blocks from tool results, silently dropping image content blocks
- ACP clients (Feishu, Discord, etc.) could not display images produced by tools (e.g. the Read tool reading PNG/JPG files) because image blocks were never forwarded
- Added image block extraction with validation (`data` and `mimeType` must both be present), using the existing `ImageContent` type from `@agentclientprotocol/sdk`
- No protocol changes needed — the ACP SDK already supports `ImageContent` in `ToolCallContent`

## Test plan

- [x] Added unit tests for `extractToolCallContent()` covering:
  - Text-only extraction (existing behavior)
  - Image-only extraction
  - Mixed text + image blocks
  - Malformed image blocks (missing data/mimeType) are skipped
  - Empty content returns undefined
  - String input fallback
- [ ] Manual verification: use ACP bridge with a Feishu/Discord client, have the agent read a PNG file, confirm the image is displayed in the client

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(ui): sync message delete to session transcript backend
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Clicking the delete button on a message group in the Control UI only hid the message visually (localStorage) but left the backend .jsonl transcript untouched. The AI could still see the "deleted" content on the next turn.
- **Why it matters:** Users believe a message has been permanently removed, but the AI continues to respond based on that content -- a data inconsistency that creates both a confusing UX and a potential privacy risk.
- **What changed:** Added `sessions.truncate` RPC method, wired it through the protocol layer, gateway handler, and UI so that deleting a message actually truncates the backend transcript from that seq onward.
- **What did NOT change:** Existing `sessions.compact`, `sessions.reset`, or any other session mutation paths are untouched.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Security hardening

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX

## Linked Issue/PR

- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History

- **Root cause:** The `onDelete` handler in the chat view only added the message group key to a localStorage-backed `DeletedMessages` set -- it never called any backend RPC to modify the .jsonl transcript.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** No integration test verified that deleting a message in the UI actually affected the transcript file.
- **Prior context:** The `sessions.compact` and `sessions.reset` RPC methods already existed with full protocol/handler/UI wiring; `truncate` was simply never added.
- **Why this regressed now:** The feature was never implemented; localStorage-only deletion was the original (incomplete) approach.

## Changes

### Backend (`src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.ts`)

Three-phase truncate with full safety:

1. **Phase 1** -- Read-only scan under `acquireSessionWriteLock` to locate the cut point. ENOENT-guarded. Does NOT abort active runs yet.
2. **Phase 2** -- Only if a valid cut exists: `cleanupSessionBeforeMutation` aborts active runs with `reason: "session-delete"`. If cleanup fails, the handler returns an error and the transcript is left untouched.
3. **Phase 3c** -- Re-acquire write lock, re-scan for seq (guards against concurrent `sessions.compact`), write-then-rename: writes truncated content to `.tmp.*`, renames original to `.bak.*`, renames temp to original. Preserves original file mode (`statSync(filePath).mode & 0o777`).

Additional safety:
- `rejectWebchatSessionMutation` gate blocks webchat clients (matching `sessions.patch`/`sessions.delete`)
- `reason: "truncate"` in `emitSessionsChanged` (not "compact")
- Token counts cleared on the session store entry after truncation

### Protocol layer

- `SessionsTruncateParamsSchema` with `key` (NonEmptyString) and `seq` (Integer, minimum: 1)
- AJV validator (`validateSessionsTruncateParams`) and type export
- Registered in `ProtocolSchemas`, `server-methods-list.ts`, and `method-scopes.ts` (operator.write)

### UI (`ui/src/ui/app-render.ts` + `ui/src/ui/views/chat.ts`)

- `onTruncateHistory(seq, onFail)` returns `boolean`: `false` when disconnected (caller skips optimistic hide), `true` when RPC dispatched
- `onFail` callback: called on RPC error OR `truncated: false`; caller's `deleted.restore(item.key)` reverses the optimistic hide
- Session-scoped: captures `state.sessionKey` at dispatch time; ignores `.then`/`.catch` if user navigated away
- Run-ID aware: captures `state.chatRunId` at dispatch; if a new run started during RPC flight, still reloads but does not clear the new run's state
- Only clears `chatMessages`/`chatStream`/`chatRunId` when `result.truncated === true`
- `.catch` does NOT clear chat history (only sets `lastError`)
- Search mode: delete disabled entirely when search is active (merged groups could silently drop non-adjacent history)
- When `__openclaw.seq` metadata is absent (e.g. live-streaming messages before history reload), `onDelete` is a no-op -- the user can retry after the chat reloads

### Swift models

- `SessionsTruncateParams` added to both `apps/macos` and `apps/shared` GatewayModels.swift

## Review Issues Addressed

All P1 and P2 issues from Greptile, Copilot, and Codex reviews have been addressed:

| # | Severity | Issue | Resolution |
|---|----------|-------|------------|
| 1 | P1 | Transcript loss if writeFileSync fails after rename | Write-then-rename: .tmp.* then .bak.* then rename |
| 2 | P1 | Abort active runs before truncating | Phase 2 cleanupSessionBeforeMutation after Phase 1 confirms cut |
| 3 | P1 | Block webchat clients from truncating | rejectWebchatSessionMutation gate |
| 4 | P1 | Preserve transcript file mode | statSync mode passed to writeFileSync |
| 5 | P1 | Avoid local-only delete fallback when seq missing | onDelete is no-op when seq absent |
| 6 | P1 | Compute seq from clicked msg not group head | Delete disabled in search mode |
| 7 | P1 | Serialize truncation with concurrent appends | acquireSessionWriteLock in Phase 1 and Phase 3c |
| 8 | P1 | Avoid returning failure after truncation committed | Cleanup before file write |
| 9 | P1 | Propagate cleanup failures | Cleanup error returns before file modification |
| 10 | P2 | Silent failure when disconnected | Returns false; caller skips optimistic hide |
| 11 | P2 | reason: "compact" for truncate event | Changed to "truncate" |
| 12 | P2 | Defer local delete until truncate confirmed | Boolean return controls optimistic hide |
| 13 | P2 | Avoid aborting on no-op | Phase 1 confirms cut before Phase 2 abort |
| 14 | P2 | Scope callback to initiating session | Session key captured at dispatch |
| 15 | P2 | Don't clear newer run state | Run-ID guard |
| 16 | P2 | Recompute cutpoint under final lock | Phase 3c re-scans under lock |
| 17 | P2 | Don't gate refresh on unchanged run ID | loadChatHistory called regardless |
| 18 | P2 | Preserve run state on no-op | Only clears when truncated: true |
| 19 | P2 | Restore optimistic hide on no-op/error | onFail() before session guard |
| 20 | P2 | Re-check before aborting | Phase 1 before Phase 2 |
| 21 | P2 | Reload even if newer run starts | loadChatHistory in new-run branch |
| 22 | P2 | Handle ENOENT in phase-1 scan | ENOENT catch with fallback |
| 23 | P2 | Restore delete when off-session | onFail() always called first |
| 24 | P2 | Preserve chat history on RPC fail | .catch only sets lastError |
| 25 | P2 | Comment refers to fromSeq | Updated to p.seq |

## Regression Test Plan

- All 30 method-scope classification tests pass
- All 37 protocol tests pass
- All 38 gateway sessions integration tests pass
- `pnpm build` passes cleanly
- `pnpm format` clean

target.title: fix(gateway): survive transient network/TLS errors in uncaughtException handler
target.body:
## Summary

The `uncaughtException` handler exits unconditionally on any error, but transient network/TLS errors (e.g. `Client network socket disconnected before secure TLS connection was established`) are non-fatal I/O failures that should not crash the gateway.

The `unhandledRejection` handler already classifies these via `isTransientNetworkError()` and continues gracefully — this PR applies the same logic to `uncaughtException` for symmetry.

### Changes

- **`src/index.ts`**: Add `isTransientNetworkError()` and `isAbortError()` checks to the `uncaughtException` handler, logging a warning instead of exiting. These errors occur in the I/O layer and do not corrupt application state, so continuing is safe.
- **`src/gateway/server-http.ts`**: Attach a fallback `error` listener on raw upgrade sockets to prevent TLS errors from bubbling up as uncaught exceptions in the first place.

## Test plan

- [x] Existing `isTransientNetworkError` / `isAbortError` unit tests pass (33/33)
- [x] All pre-commit checks (lint, format, type-check, boundary checks) pass
- [ ] Verify gateway no longer crashes when network is unstable (manual test with network interruption)

Closes #36585
Closes #35257

target.title: feat(feishu): support im.message.recall_v1 event
target.body:
## Problem
Feishu recall events are not handled — bot may reply to recalled messages or leave orphan replies.
## Solution
- Subscribe to im.message.recall_v1 in Feishu Open Platform
- Track user_message_id → bot_reply_message_id mapping
- On recall: delete bot reply messages
- On pre-reply check: verify message still exists
## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Bot skips reply if user recalls before bot responds
- [ ] Bot deletes reply if user recalls after bot responds
- [ ] Duplicate recalls handled idempotently
- [ ] One-to-many replies (multiple bot msgs per user msg) all deleted

target.title: CLI: add stable setup surface export for dynamic provider/channel configuration UIs
target.body:
# CLI: add a stable setup surface export for dynamic provider/channel configuration UIs

## Summary

This PR adds a stable, machine-readable setup surface export for OpenClaw so external UIs can render provider and channel configuration dynamically without importing internal runtime chunks or scraping interactive CLI output.

Today, setup metadata already exists across:

- plugin manifests
- provider auth choices
- channel setup wizards
- setup-only plugin entries

But OpenClaw does not yet expose a supported serialized contract for exporting that setup information.
As a result, downstream UIs either hardcode a small subset of flows or depend on brittle internal implementation details.

This PR adds a minimal, stable, read-only export seam without changing the behavior of existing `setup` or `configure` commands.

## Problem

An external configuration UI that wants to track OpenClaw's real supported surface needs to know:

- which providers and channels are available
- how they should be labeled and ordered
- whether they are installed or configured
- which fields they require
- whether setup is token-based, env-based, browser/oauth-based, polling-based, or multi-step
- which status, hints, and docs should be displayed

Current interfaces are not enough:

- `openclaw plugins list --json` is plugin inventory, not setup surface
- `openclaw configure` is interactive, not machine-readable
- importing internal `dist` chunks is unstable across builds and versions
- current setup wizards are executable JS objects, not a public JSON contract

That leaves downstream UIs stuck with two weak choices:

- hardcode provider/channel flows
- depend on internal implementation details that may break on upgrade

## Proposal

Add a dedicated setup surface export command.

Suggested form:

- `openclaw configure surface --json-out <file>`

Optional flags may include:

- `--section providers|channels`
- `--installed-only`
- `--workspace <dir>`

This command writes a complete setup surface document to a JSON file for external UIs to consume.

A key design principle is:

- machine consumers must not depend on clean stdout
- the supported protocol should be the output file

That avoids JSON corruption from mixed logs or warnings.

## Why this is low risk

This PR:

- does not change existing `openclaw setup`
- does not change existing `openclaw configure`
- does not change existing plugin setup behavior
- only adds a read-only export seam
- allows external UIs to stop depending on internal chunk exports

This is not a rewrite of configure/onboarding. It is a formal export for setup surfaces that already exist.

## Why this fits the plugin/extensibility model

This is not an app-specific special case. It formalizes OpenClaw's existing plugin-defined setup capabilities.

In other words:

- channel plugins continue to define setup via `ChannelSetupWizard` and setup entries
- provider plugins continue to define auth/setup via provider auth choices and provider metadata
- core only serializes those existing setup surfaces into stable descriptors
- any UI can consume the same export, not just one client

This strengthens the plugin architecture and makes plugin-defined setup surfaces reusable.

## Suggested exported content

For each provider or channel, export:

- `id`
- `kind`
  - `provider` or `channel`
- `label`
- `detailLabel`
- `selectionLabel`
- `blurb`
- `docsPath`
- `systemImage` when available
- `installed`
- `configured`
- `selectionHint`
- `statusLines`
- `quickstartScore`
- `fields`
  - serializable field descriptors derived from credentials, text inputs, env shortcuts, etc.
- `actions`
  - serializable action descriptors such as submit, open browser, poll, install plugin, etc.
- `capabilities`
  - for example multi-account, oauth, polling, env shortcut, gateway-required

The focus of this PR is exporting setup surface descriptors, not rewriting setup execution semantics.

## Non-goals

This PR does not attempt to:

- replace existing interactive `configure`
- redesign setup wizard internals
- expose setup wizard JS objects directly as public API
- expose unstable internal runtime module layouts
- add app-specific behavior for a single client

## Implementation approach

Minimal implementation path:

1. Define serializable setup surface descriptor types.
2. Implement an exporter that builds descriptors from existing provider/channel setup metadata.
3. Add a `configure surface` command.
4. Support `--json-out`.
5. Keep logs on stderr and let machine consumers rely only on the output file.

## Why `--json-out` instead of stdout-only JSON

In some environments, stdout may include logs, warnings, or diagnostics.
If the protocol depends on perfectly clean stdout, integrations become brittle.

File output is a better long-term machine interface because it avoids:

- stdout contamination
- JSON extraction/cleanup heuristics
- inconsistent behavior across environments

For that reason, this PR proposes:

- `--json-out <file>`

as the supported machine interface.

## Value for downstream UIs

With this export, external UIs can:

- render all providers dynamically
- render all channels dynamically
- stop maintaining hardcoded setup lists
- track OpenClaw's supported surface across upgrades

This is especially useful for onboarding flows and settings dashboards.

## Compatibility

- existing CLI usage remains unchanged
- existing plugins do not need immediate changes
- older clients can ignore the new command completely
- newer clients can depend on the formal seam instead of internal implementation details

## Why this is better than client-side workarounds

Without this seam, downstream clients are forced to choose between:

- hardcoded setup UIs
- importing internal chunks
- parsing partial plugin inventory
- scraping interactive CLI output

All of these are weaker contracts than adding a small, explicit export interface.

## Possible follow-ups

This PR is intentionally the smallest useful step. Future work can build on it with:

- richer provider setup surface export
- setup session execution APIs
- gateway/RPC access to setup surfaces
- more formal plugin-sdk public types

But this PR itself stays intentionally narrow:

- a stable, read-only, machine-consumable setup surface export

target.title: feat(routing): support per-binding workspace override
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds an optional `workspace` field to route bindings so that the same agent can operate in different directories depending on which chat/peer it responds to.
- Threads the workspace through `ResolvedAgentRoute` so downstream consumers can use it.
- In `getReplyFromConfig`, binding workspace takes precedence over the agent-level default workspace.

### Motivation

In git-worktree workflows, each worktree lives in a separate directory but logically belongs to the same agent. Currently, the only way to point an agent at a different directory is to create a separate agent entry per workspace. With per-binding workspace, a single agent definition can serve multiple worktrees — each bound to a different chat/group via the existing binding mechanism.

**Example config:**
```json
{
  "bindings": [{
    "agentId": "coder",
    "workspace": "/path/to/feature-worktree-A",
    "match": { "channel": "slack", "peer": { "kind": "group", "id": "C_CHANNEL_A" } }
  }, {
    "agentId": "coder",
    "workspace": "/path/to/feature-worktree-B",
    "match": { "channel": "slack", "peer": { "kind": "group", "id": "C_CHANNEL_B" } }
  }]
}
```

### Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/config/types.agents.ts` | Add `workspace?: string` to `AgentRouteBinding` |
| `src/config/zod-schema.agents.ts` | Add `workspace` to `RouteBindingSchema` |
| `src/routing/resolve-route.ts` | Thread `workspace` through `ResolvedAgentRoute` and `choose()` |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply.ts` | Add `resolveBindingWorkspace()` — binding workspace takes precedence |

## Test plan

- [ ] Verify existing routing tests pass (`pnpm test -- --grep routing`)
- [ ] Add a binding with `workspace` field and confirm the agent runs in that directory
- [ ] Confirm bindings without `workspace` behave identically to before (no regression)
- [ ] Verify `resolveBindingWorkspace` returns `undefined` when no binding matches


Made with [Cursor](https://cursor.com)
