commands.author: Jerry-Xin

target.title: fix(cron): skip deleteAfterRun and preserve counters for manual runs (#83538)
target.body:
## Summary

`openclaw cron run` (manual/debug runs) on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes the job on success — consuming the one-shot and preventing its scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate `consecutiveErrors`/`consecutiveSkipped` counters, interfering with scheduled retry/backoff state.

This PR adds an `isManual` flag to `applyJobResult` so manual runs preserve the job and leave scheduled execution state untouched.

## Root cause

`applyJobResult()` has no concept of manual vs scheduled runs. When a manual run succeeds on a `deleteAfterRun: true` at-job, `shouldDelete` evaluates to `true` and the job is removed from the store — the scheduled fire never happens.

Additionally, manual error/skipped/success outcomes unconditionally mutate `consecutiveErrors` and `consecutiveSkipped` counters, which control retry backoff and auto-disable behavior for scheduled runs.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `timer.ts` | Add `isManual` option to `applyJobResult`; skip `deleteAfterRun` for manual runs; guard all counter mutations and alert emissions with `!isManual` |
| `ops.ts` | Pass `isManual: true` from manual run completion and invalid-persisted-run handling |
| `timer.regression.test.ts` | 5 new tests: manual ok/error preserve job, manual doesn't increment/reset counters |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts
 ✓ cron (45 tests) 144ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  45 passed (45)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes it, preventing scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate scheduled retry counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `aa92a4a5b0`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0.

### Source-level proof

```
Main branch applyJobResult():
  - shouldDelete = kind === "at" && deleteAfterRun && status === "ok"
    → no manual discriminator, always deletes on success
  - consecutiveErrors incremented unconditionally on error
  - consecutiveSkipped incremented unconditionally on skip

Fix branch:
  - shouldDelete = wouldDelete && !opts?.isManual
    → manual runs never trigger deletion
  - Counter mutations guarded: if (!opts?.isManual) { ... }
    → manual runs don't affect retry/backoff state
```

### Regression test coverage

```
✅ manual ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted
✅ manual error on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted  
✅ scheduled ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job deleted (unchanged behavior)
✅ manual error does not increment consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
✅ manual success does not reset consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
```

- **What was not tested**: Live `openclaw cron run` CLI flow (requires a running gateway with a configured `deleteAfterRun` at-job). The `isManual` flag propagation from `ops.ts` to `applyJobResult` is validated at the unit level.

Closes #83538

target.title: feat(feishu): expose reaction tool
target.body:
# PR 2 draft: Feishu reaction tool registration

Title:

```text
feat(feishu): expose reaction tool
```

## Summary

- Add an agent-callable `feishu_reaction` tool with `add`, `remove`, and `list` actions.
- Wire the tool through Feishu tool config defaults, config validation, account-level tool merging, plugin metadata, API exports, and bundled entry registration.
- Normalize common emoji aliases such as `👍`, `heart`, `clap`, and `ok` to Feishu emoji type strings before calling the reactions API.
- Preserve canonical Feishu mixed-case emoji type strings such as `Typing` instead of uppercasing unknown values.
- Honor the existing `actions.reactions` policy gate as well as the new `tools.reactions` tool-registration gate.
- Document the Feishu-native reaction tool and `channels.feishu.tools.reactions` gate.

## Related upstream work

- Supersedes https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/39567
- Addresses the missing reaction-tool portion of https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/8692
- Complements the lower-level reaction bug fixes in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/66406 and https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/66478
- Keeps scope narrow and does not change the existing generic `message(action="react")` path, reducing overlap with https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/34528 and https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/77653

## Notes for reviewers

- This PR registers the already-existing Feishu reactions API surface as an agent tool.
- `tools.reactions` defaults to `false`; the native tool is opt-in because generic `message(action="react")` already covers Feishu reactions.
- `actions.reactions: false` disables both generic reaction actions and this native tool path.
- `remove` accepts either a Feishu `reaction_id` or an `emoji_type`; when an emoji type is provided, the tool removes the bot/app-owned matching reaction.
- Native and generic reaction removal now share ownership-safe helpers; emoji normalization for the native tool preserves unknown/canonical Feishu values.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Feishu agents can use the native `feishu_reaction` tool to add, list, and remove emoji reactions on real Feishu messages instead of having the reactions API implemented but unreachable from agents.

Real environment tested: Windows 11 host, local OpenClaw checkout on this PR branch, enterprise self-built Feishu app using WebSocket mode, real Feishu group chat with the bot added, and Feishu app scope `im:message:reactions:write_only` enabled.

5. Used the latest real Feishu `message_id` from the local gateway dedup state as the target.
6. Executed `remove OK` pre-clean, then `add OK`, `list OK`, `remove OK`, and final `list OK`.

Evidence after fix:
Copied terminal output from the live command. IDs are redacted; no app secret/token is included.

target.title: docs(providers): merge GLM docs into Z.AI page
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Z.AI and GLM are actually the same provider in OpenClaw (`zai` provider, `ZAI_API_KEY`, Z.AI Chat Completions / Bearer auth), but the docs were split across `/providers/zai` (provider page) and `/providers/glm` (model-family page). The two pages duplicated setup guidance, drifted apart over time, and users entering from either side had to bounce between them to get the full picture.
- **Solution:** Merge the GLM page into `docs/providers/zai.md`, delete `docs/providers/glm.md`, and point every internal link / nav entry / i18n nav / redirect at `/providers/zai`. While merging, add a Property table and a Config example to the Z.AI page, and simplify Getting started from 3 steps to 2 (onboarding already sets the default model, so the "Set a default model" step is no longer needed).
- **What changed:**
  - `docs/providers/zai.md`: add Property table; add Config example section; correct the auto-detect wording (the `zai-api-key` choice **probes** supported endpoints with the API key — it does not infer from a key prefix); simplify Getting started from 3 to 2 steps; align Related cards with Model selection / Configuration reference.
  - Delete `docs/providers/glm.md` (content merged into `zai.md`). The legacy env var `Z_AI_API_KEY` (which the runtime still accepts via `src/infra/env.ts` and lists in `extensions/zai/openclaw.plugin.json`) is preserved in the consolidated docs — see the auth row of the Property table and the Auth details accordion.
  - `docs/docs.json`: change `/glm` destination from `/providers/glm` to `/providers/zai`; add new `/providers/glm` → `/providers/zai` redirect; remove `providers/glm` from navigation.
  - `docs/concepts/model-providers.md`: keep main's `<CardGroup>` structure; rename the GLM card to `Z.AI (GLM)` and update href to `/providers/zai`.
  - `docs/providers/index.md`, `docs/providers/models.md`, `docs/concepts/oauth.md`, `docs/help/faq-first-run.md`, `docs/providers/anthropic.md`, `docs/.i18n/zh-Hans-navigation.json`: every reference to `glm` / `providers/glm` updated to `providers/zai`.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** Pure docs. No changes to runtime / `extensions/zai` / `src/plugins/provider-zai-endpoint.ts` / any plugin / CI / config schema. No standalone stub page kept at `/providers/glm` — the redirect in `docs.json` handles it.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

(Docs-only PR. UI / DX is checked because this is the first-touch onboarding doc for the Z.AI/GLM provider and directly shapes the new-user setup flow.)

## Linked Issue/PR

No prior issue — submitted as a direct contribution.

- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

The motivation comes from a real onboarding gap: many entry points (`docs/concepts/model-providers.md`, `docs/providers/index.md`, `docs/concepts/oauth.md`, `docs/help/faq-first-run.md`) surface `GLM models` first and route users to `/providers/glm`, which had its own self-contained setup. But the actual OpenClaw provider is `zai` (`ZAI_API_KEY`, Z.AI Chat Completions), so half of the operational information also lived under `/providers/zai`. Over time the two pages drifted, and "GLM is a model family hosted by Z.AI" — a key conceptual point — was hard to express cleanly across two separate pages. Consolidating to a single Z.AI page makes that relationship explicit at the top of the page and removes a recurring source of new-user confusion. The merge also lets us reflect the current onboarding behavior accurately: `openclaw onboard --auth-choice zai-api-key` (and the regional `zai-coding-*` / `zai-cn` variants) already sets the default model, so the old "Set a default model" doc step is now stale and is removed in this PR.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Doc split between Z.AI and GLM produced two competing entry points and stale links. After the merge, every legacy URL (`/glm`, `/providers/glm`, `/zai`) must redirect to `/providers/zai`; the Z.AI page must render the full GLM model catalog and the merged Getting started; nav / providers index must not show a standalone GLM entry.
- **Real environment tested:** Local Mintlify dev preview. `npx --yes mintlify dev` on Linux, Node v24.15.0, started from `docs/` in a clean checkout of this branch. `docs/docs.json` uses Mintlify schema `https://mintlify.com/docs.json`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```bash
  cd docs
  npx --yes mintlify dev

  # In another terminal, once the preview is ready on localhost:3000:
  curl -sI http://localhost:3000/glm           | grep -iE 'HTTP|location'
  curl -sI http://localhost:3000/providers/glm | grep -iE 'HTTP|location'
  curl -sI http://localhost:3000/zai           | grep -iE 'HTTP|location'
  curl -sI http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -iE 'HTTP'
  curl -s   http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -oiE '<title[^>]*>[^<]*</title>'
  curl -s   http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -oiE 'glm-[0-9.a-z-]+|z\.ai|coding[- ]plan' | sort -u
  curl -s   http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -oE 'Z_AI_API_KEY' | wc -l
  ```
- **Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):**

  Terminal output from the local Mintlify preview (verbatim):

  ```
  $ curl -sI http://localhost:3000/glm | grep -iE 'HTTP|location'
  HTTP/1.1 307 Temporary Redirect
  location: /providers/zai

  $ curl -sI http://localhost:3000/providers/glm | grep -iE 'HTTP|location'
  HTTP/1.1 307 Temporary Redirect
  location: /providers/zai

  $ curl -sI http://localhost:3000/zai | grep -iE 'HTTP|location'
  HTTP/1.1 307 Temporary Redirect
  location: /providers/zai

  $ curl -sI http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -iE 'HTTP'
  HTTP/1.1 200 OK

  $ curl -s http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -oiE '<title[^>]*>[^<]*</title>'
  <title>Z.AI - OpenClaw</title>

  $ curl -s http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -oiE 'glm-[0-9.a-z-]+|z\.ai|coding[- ]plan' | sort -u
  Coding Plan
  GLM-5
  Z.AI
  glm-4.5
  glm-4.5-air
  glm-4.5-flash
  glm-4.5v
  glm-4.6
  glm-4.6v
  glm-4.7
  glm-4.7-flash
  glm-4.7-flashx
  glm-5
  glm-5-models
  glm-5-turbo
  glm-5.1
  glm-5v-turbo
  glm-models-naming-
  z.ai

  $ curl -s http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -oE 'Z_AI_API_KEY' | wc -l
  6
  ```

  The 6 occurrences of `Z_AI_API_KEY` cover the auth row of the Property table, the "Auth details" accordion bullet, and the rendered React payload — confirming the legacy env var is documented in both prominent spots after the merge.

- **Observed result after fix:**
  - `/glm`, `/providers/glm`, and `/zai` all return `307 Temporary Redirect` with `location: /providers/zai`. (Production Mintlify may use a different redirect status, but the destination is the same and behavior is equivalent.)
  - `/providers/zai` returns `200 OK` with `<title>Z.AI - OpenClaw</title>`.
  - The rendered HTML contains the full GLM model list (`glm-4.5-air`, `glm-4.5-flash`, `glm-4.7-flash`, `glm-4.7-flashx`, `glm-5-turbo`, `glm-5-models`, `glm-models`) and `Z.AI` / `Coding Plan` sections — the original `glm.md` content has been merged in without loss.
  - Mintlify dev starts with no broken-link warnings; all previous links to `providers/glm` have been updated to `providers/zai`.
- **What was not tested:** Production Mintlify CDN cache. If any stale `/providers/glm` cache objects exist on the production deployment, they will either expire naturally or need to be purged on the deployment side — out of scope for this PR.
- **Before evidence (optional but encouraged):** The "before" state can be observed on the current production deployment: https://docs.openclaw.ai/providers/glm and https://docs.openclaw.ai/providers/zai are still two separate pages with duplicated setup that has drifted apart.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Visitors to `/providers/glm` or `/glm` are redirected to `/providers/zai`. (`/glm` previously redirected to `/providers/glm`; the destination is updated. `/providers/glm` is a new redirect.)
- The docs site navigation and providers index no longer show a standalone `GLM models` entry. `Z.AI` is the single entry point and its subtitle mentions GLM.
- The Z.AI page now has a Property table and a Config example. Getting started is simplified from 3 steps (onboard → set default model → verify) to 2 steps (onboard → verify), reflecting that onboarding already sets the default model.
- The endpoint auto-detect description is corrected: the `zai-api-key` choice probes supported endpoints with the API key, rather than inferring from a key prefix.
- The legacy `Z_AI_API_KEY` env var (still accepted by the runtime; documented previously only on the deleted GLM page) is now documented on the consolidated Z.AI page — in the Property table auth row and the Auth details accordion. No behavior change for users who only set `ZAI_API_KEY`.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No**
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **No**
- Data access scope changed? **No**

Docs-only — no runtime / auth / network behavior changes.

## Repro + Verification

See §Real behavior proof above — Environment, Steps, Expected, and Actual are all covered there.

- OS: Linux (Ubuntu)
- Runtime/container: Node v24.15.0; `npx --yes mintlify dev` (no global install)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): `docs/docs.json` (Mintlify schema)

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets — see Real behavior proof / Evidence after fix
- [ ] Screenshot/recording — optional; terminal evidence is sufficient
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - All three legacy URLs (`/glm`, `/providers/glm`, `/zai`) correctly redirect to `/providers/zai` in the local Mintlify preview.
  - The rendered `/providers/zai` page contains the full GLM model list and the merged Getting started content.
  - Mintlify dev starts with no broken-link warnings.
  - Branch hygiene: ahead 1 commit relative to `origin/main`, diff limited to 10 files under `docs/`, no runtime / extensions / UI / superpowers files leaked into the PR.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Both `/glm` (existing redirect, destination updated) and `/providers/glm` (newly added redirect) paths are exercised.
  - The Chinese navigation `docs/.i18n/zh-Hans-navigation.json` also has `zh-CN/providers/glm` removed.
- What was **not** verified:
  - CDN cache purge on the production docs.openclaw.ai deployment (deployment-side concern).
  - Whether every GLM model id string in the page still corresponds to a callable model on Z.AI today — this PR only consolidates docs and does not change the bundled `zai` manifest.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes** — legacy URLs still resolve via redirects.
- Config/env changes? **No**
- Migration needed? **No**

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: If the production Mintlify deployment has CDN-cached `/providers/glm` responses, users may see stale content briefly.
  - Mitigation: Cached entries will expire naturally, or a maintainer can purge them on the deployment side. The redirect itself is in `docs.json` and takes effect immediately once cache is cleared.
- Risk: External blog posts or old GitHub issues may still link to `/providers/glm`.
  - Mitigation: The `docs.json` redirect covers both `/providers/glm` and `/glm`, so external links continue to land users on the consolidated page. No external-link fix-up needed.

commands.author: Jerry-Xin

target.title: fix(infra): tolerate chmod EPERM on K8s fsGroup-mounted PVC
target.body:
## Summary

On Kubernetes deployments with `fsGroup`-mounted PVCs, `chmod(dir, 0o700)` fails with EPERM because the state directory is owned by root (with group ownership set to the fsGroup GID). This crashes exec approvals, task registry, task-flow registry, and plugin-state initialization on startup.

This PR extracts a shared `tryChmodSync` helper that:
1. Tries `chmodSync(target, mode)` as before
2. On EPERM/EACCES/EROFS: verifies the process can actually access the path via `accessSync`
3. If accessible → tolerates the failure (the fsGroup grants adequate permissions via group bits)
4. If not accessible → rethrows the original error

## Root cause

```
EPERM: operation not permitted, chmod '/home/openclaw/.openclaw'
```

K8s `fsGroup` volumes are owned by `root:<fsGroup>` with setgid bit. The container process runs as a non-root UID in the fsGroup, so it has full access via group membership but cannot `chmodSync` a root-owned directory.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/infra/chmod.ts` | New `tryChmodSync` helper — chmod with EPERM/EACCES/EROFS tolerance |
| `src/infra/chmod.test.ts` | 9 tests covering all error codes and access verification paths |
| `src/infra/exec-approvals.ts` | Use `tryChmodSync` instead of raw `chmodSync` |
| `src/tasks/task-registry.store.sqlite.ts` | Use `tryChmodSync` |
| `src/tasks/task-flow-registry.store.sqlite.ts` | Use `tryChmodSync` |
| `src/plugin-state/plugin-state-store.sqlite.ts` | Use `tryChmodSync` |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/chmod.test.ts
 ✓ infra (9 tests) 38ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  9 passed (9)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `chmod(dir, 0o700)` throws EPERM on K8s fsGroup-mounted PVC, crashing exec approvals and state initialization.
- **Real environment tested**: OrbStack Kubernetes v1.33.9, Node.js v24.15.0, macOS arm64 host.

### Live K8s reproduction

Pod spec:
```yaml
securityContext:
  runAsUser: 1000
  runAsGroup: 1000
  fsGroup: 1000
volumes:
  - name: state
    emptyDir: {}  # simulates fsGroup PVC
```

Terminal output from `kubectl exec`:
```
=== K8s fsGroup EPERM Reproduction ===
Node: v24.15.0
User: uid=1000(node) gid=1000(node) groups=1000(node)

--- State directory (fsGroup mount) ---
drwxrwsrwx 1 root node  0 May 20 07:00 .openclaw

Owner: 0:1000
Mode: 0o2777

--- Main branch: chmod(dir, 0o700) ---
chmod FAILED: EPERM EPERM: operation not permitted, chmod '/home/openclaw/.openclaw'

--- PR fix: tryChmodSync(dir, 0o700) ---
chmod failed with EPERM - checking access...
accessSync(R_OK|W_OK|X_OK) PASSED ✅
→ tryChmodSync tolerates EPERM, gateway starts normally

--- Verify: can create state files ---
Write+Read: ✅ {"test":true}
Cleanup: ✅
```

**Before fix**: `chmodSync` throws EPERM → gateway crashes on startup.
**After fix**: `tryChmodSync` catches EPERM, verifies access via `accessSync(R_OK|W_OK|X_OK)`, tolerates the failure. State files can be read/written normally.

- **What was not tested**: Full OpenClaw gateway startup inside K8s (would require building and deploying a custom image). The chmod behavior is validated end-to-end in the K8s environment.

Closes #83619

target.title: fix: block empty exec commands + consecutive tool-error circuit breaker (plus minor UI test stability)
target.body:
**Summary**
- Add a guard that blocks `exec`/`bash` tool calls when the `command` parameter is missing or blank, preventing empty-command execution loops.
- Add a consecutive tool-error counter and a circuit-breaker callback in the embedded subscribe tool handler chain; trip after 3 consecutive tool errors.
- Stabilize UI `formatNextRun()` weekday output across locales so tests don’t fail on non-English environments.

**Motivation / Context**
- Following `exec-loop-fix.md`, we observed cases where empty `exec` commands could lead to ineffective retry/loop behavior.
- We implement two layers of protection:
  1) pre-call validation to reject empty command invocations,
  2) runtime circuit breaker to stop repeated tool failures and surface a deterministic failure signal.
- Additionally, UI tests were flaky on systems whose default locale is not English (weekday formatting output differed).

**Implementation Details**
- **Layer 1 (pre-tool-call guard)**  
  - Normalize tool name (`exec`/`bash`) and validate params.
  - If `command` is missing/empty/whitespace-only, return `{ blocked: true, reason }` and log a warning, preventing the call from running.
- **Layer 2 (consecutive-error circuit breaker)**  
  - Introduce state tracking for consecutive tool errors and an `onConsecutiveToolError` callback.
  - Increment on tool failures and trip after 3 consecutive errors; invoke the callback to stop/redirect the flow.
- **UI test stability**  
  - `formatNextRun()` now formats the weekday with a fixed locale (`en-US`) to make output deterministic across environments.

**Key Files Changed**
- `src/agents/pi-tools.before-tool-call.ts` (block empty exec/bash command)
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.types.ts` (error-state types)
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.types.ts` (callback type)
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.ts` (state initialization)
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.ts` (counter + threshold logic)
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts` (runner integration)

target.title: fix: normalize visible assistant output before delivery
target.body:
## Summary
Gemma was sometimes leaking hidden internal text into Discord replies.
This PR makes OpenClaw clean the final reply in one shared place before sending it.
It adds regression tests for the real bad cases we saw, including leaked `<channel|>` markers and runaway repeated replies.
The fix is shared, not Gemma-only, so every delivery path uses the same cleanup.


## What changed
- teach the shared visible-text normalizer to:
  - strip leaked `<channel|>` control delimiters when they behave like leaked scaffolding
  - preserve literal `<channel|>` mentions in ordinary prose
  - preserve explicitly requested quoted targets, including full repeated strings and visible outputs that end with `<channel|>`
  - honor explicit repeat-count requests like `repeat "ABCD-1234" exactly twice`
  - drop truncated trailing leaked delimiters after long internal preambles
  - keep real leaks visible even when the preamble previously mentioned `<channel|>` literally
  - prefer the minimal repeated unit when a no-delimiter runaway answer repeats a mistakenly doubled exact target many times
  - preserve meaningful leading indentation in delivery mode while still trimming incidental single-space padding
- apply delivery-mode sanitization in the final delivery paths, while keeping stream-update sanitization lighter
- defer chunked `text_end` delivery until the final flush so partial raw scaffold chunks are never emitted early
- mark chunked `message_end` drains as final delivery so the delivery sanitizer runs on the last emitted chunks
- preserve plain streamed chunks without directive parsing when no inline directives are present
- preserve legacy `text_end` reply directives and keep intermediate chunk drains in history mode until final delivery
- reset `blockState` when `text_end` replaces the block buffer with already-sanitized final text, so stale reasoning-tag state cannot strip the final answer on flush

## Regression tests added first
- long prose that literally mentions `<channel|>` stays intact
- `plan: <channel|>visible` is treated as leaked scaffolding
- explicitly requested repeated outputs stay intact when named literally, in quotes, or via repeat-count instructions
- explicitly requested visible outputs that end with `<channel|>` stay intact
- literal marker explanations after an initial leaked delimiter stay intact
- truncated leaked delimiters after long preambles are dropped
- leaked delimiters are still stripped when earlier preamble text mentioned `<channel|>` literally
- repeated exact-target salvage prefers the minimal unit when a no-delimiter runaway answer duplicates the target in the model preamble and then repeats it many times
- legacy `text_end` reply directives still work
- chunked `message_end` drains are marked as final delivery
- intermediate chunk drains keep indentation before `text_end`
- delivery-mode sanitization keeps meaningful leading indentation while still trimming incidental padding
- chunk-boundary reasoning tags do not eat the final flushed answer

## Verification
- failing regressions were added first and reproduced locally before the fixes
- focused tests:
  - `pnpm exec vitest run src/shared/text/repeated-visible-suffix.test.ts src/shared/text/assistant-visible-text.test.ts`
  - `pnpm exec vitest run src/shared/text/repeated-visible-suffix.test.ts src/shared/text/assistant-visible-text.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-utils.strip-model-special-tokens.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.messages.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.reply-tags.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.emits-block-replies-text-end-does-not.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.keeps-indented-fenced-blocks-intact.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-utils.test.ts`
- `pnpm build`
- live Discord probe on `vllm/gemma4-e4b` after restarting the local gateway on this branch:
  - Looper prompt: `reply with exactly pr68986-live2-1776657698 and nothing else`
  - Bob visible Discord reply: `pr68986-live2-1776657698`

target.title: fix(telegram): honor pollingStallThresholdMs in isolated ingress mode
target.body:
## Summary

`pollingStallThresholdMs` is honored in the non-isolated Telegram polling cycle but silently ignored when isolated ingress is enabled (the default). If the isolated polling worker wedges, the watchdog never fires and the channel stays dead until a manual restart.

This PR adds the same `TelegramPollingLivenessTracker` watchdog to `#runIsolatedIngressCycle`, feeding it from worker `poll-start`, `poll-success`, and `poll-error` messages. When a stall is detected, the transport is marked dirty and the worker is stopped, triggering the existing restart recovery.

## Root cause

Current main has two polling cycles:
- **Non-isolated** (`#runPollingCycle`): has `detectStall()` watchdog ✅
- **Isolated** (`#runIsolatedIngressCycle`): no `detectStall()` watchdog ❌

Isolated ingress is **default enabled** (`opts.isolatedIngress?.enabled ?? true`), so most Telegram users run without stall detection.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `polling-session.ts` | Add liveness tracker + watchdog interval to isolated cycle; feed from worker messages |
| `polling-session.test.ts` | 3 new tests: stall triggers restart, healthy worker avoids false restart, custom threshold honored |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.test.ts
 ✓ telegram (45 tests)
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  45 passed (45)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `pollingStallThresholdMs` config has no effect when isolated ingress is enabled (the default). A wedged worker never triggers recovery.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, OpenClaw `2026.5.18`. Live Telegram bot `@thomas_fu_bot` running with default isolated ingress.

### Source gap proof (main vs fix)

```
$ grep -n "detectStall" extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.ts

Main branch:
  902: detectStall  ← only in #runPollingCycle (non-isolated)

Fix branch:
  793: detectStall  ← NEW: in #runIsolatedIngressCycle
  922: detectStall  ← existing: in #runPollingCycle
```

The fix adds `detectStall()` to the isolated cycle (line 793), matching the existing non-isolated implementation.

### Live Telegram environment verification

```
Bot: @thomas_fu_bot
Config: enabled=true, polling mode
Isolated ingress: default enabled (not overridden)
pollingStallThresholdMs: default 120_000 (2 min)
```

The local gateway runs with isolated ingress enabled by default. Before this fix, a polling stall in the isolated worker would go undetected. After this fix, the watchdog interval checks `detectStall()` every `POLL_WATCHDOG_INTERVAL_MS` and triggers worker restart on timeout.

### Unit test coverage

3 new isolated-mode tests:
1. **No worker messages → stall detected → restart** ✅
2. **Regular poll-success messages → no false restart** ✅
3. **Custom threshold honored** ✅

- **What was not tested**: Inducing a real network stall on a live Telegram bot (would require blocking api.telegram.org traffic, which risks disrupting the live bot). The stall detection logic is identical to the proven non-isolated watchdog, just applied to the isolated worker message channel.

Closes #83950

target.title: docs(providers): merge GLM docs into Z.AI page
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Z.AI and GLM are actually the same provider in OpenClaw (`zai` provider, `ZAI_API_KEY`, Z.AI Chat Completions / Bearer auth), but the docs were split across `/providers/zai` (provider page) and `/providers/glm` (model-family page). The two pages duplicated setup guidance, drifted apart over time, and users entering from either side had to bounce between them to get the full picture.
- **Solution:** Merge the GLM page into `docs/providers/zai.md`, delete `docs/providers/glm.md`, and point every internal link / nav entry / i18n nav / redirect at `/providers/zai`. While merging, add a Property table and a Config example to the Z.AI page, and simplify Getting started from 3 steps to 2 (onboarding already sets the default model, so the "Set a default model" step is no longer needed).
- **What changed:**
  - `docs/providers/zai.md`: add Property table; add Config example section; correct the auto-detect wording (the `zai-api-key` choice **probes** supported endpoints with the API key — it does not infer from a key prefix); simplify Getting started from 3 to 2 steps; align Related cards with Model selection / Configuration reference.
  - Delete `docs/providers/glm.md` (content merged into `zai.md`). The legacy env var `Z_AI_API_KEY` (which the runtime still accepts via `src/infra/env.ts` and lists in `extensions/zai/openclaw.plugin.json`) is preserved in the consolidated docs — see the auth row of the Property table and the Auth details accordion.
  - `docs/docs.json`: change `/glm` destination from `/providers/glm` to `/providers/zai`; add new `/providers/glm` → `/providers/zai` redirect; remove `providers/glm` from navigation.
  - `docs/concepts/model-providers.md`: keep main's `<CardGroup>` structure; rename the GLM card to `Z.AI (GLM)` and update href to `/providers/zai`.
  - `docs/providers/index.md`, `docs/providers/models.md`, `docs/concepts/oauth.md`, `docs/help/faq-first-run.md`, `docs/providers/anthropic.md`, `docs/.i18n/zh-Hans-navigation.json`: every reference to `glm` / `providers/glm` updated to `providers/zai`.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** Pure docs. No changes to runtime / `extensions/zai` / `src/plugins/provider-zai-endpoint.ts` / any plugin / CI / config schema. No standalone stub page kept at `/providers/glm` — the redirect in `docs.json` handles it.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

(Docs-only PR. UI / DX is checked because this is the first-touch onboarding doc for the Z.AI/GLM provider and directly shapes the new-user setup flow.)

## Linked Issue/PR

No prior issue — submitted as a direct contribution.

- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

The motivation comes from a real onboarding gap: many entry points (`docs/concepts/model-providers.md`, `docs/providers/index.md`, `docs/concepts/oauth.md`, `docs/help/faq-first-run.md`) surface `GLM models` first and route users to `/providers/glm`, which had its own self-contained setup. But the actual OpenClaw provider is `zai` (`ZAI_API_KEY`, Z.AI Chat Completions), so half of the operational information also lived under `/providers/zai`. Over time the two pages drifted, and "GLM is a model family hosted by Z.AI" — a key conceptual point — was hard to express cleanly across two separate pages. Consolidating to a single Z.AI page makes that relationship explicit at the top of the page and removes a recurring source of new-user confusion. The merge also lets us reflect the current onboarding behavior accurately: `openclaw onboard --auth-choice zai-api-key` (and the regional `zai-coding-*` / `zai-cn` variants) already sets the default model, so the old "Set a default model" doc step is now stale and is removed in this PR.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Doc split between Z.AI and GLM produced two competing entry points and stale links. After the merge, every legacy URL (`/glm`, `/providers/glm`, `/zai`) must redirect to `/providers/zai`; the Z.AI page must render the full GLM model catalog and the merged Getting started; nav / providers index must not show a standalone GLM entry.
- **Real environment tested:** Local Mintlify dev preview. `npx --yes mintlify dev` on Linux, Node v24.15.0, started from `docs/` in a clean checkout of this branch. `docs/docs.json` uses Mintlify schema `https://mintlify.com/docs.json`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```bash
  cd docs
  npx --yes mintlify dev

  # In another terminal, once the preview is ready on localhost:3000:
  curl -sI http://localhost:3000/glm           | grep -iE 'HTTP|location'
  curl -sI http://localhost:3000/providers/glm | grep -iE 'HTTP|location'
  curl -sI http://localhost:3000/zai           | grep -iE 'HTTP|location'
  curl -sI http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -iE 'HTTP'
  curl -s   http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -oiE '<title[^>]*>[^<]*</title>'
  curl -s   http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -oiE 'glm-[0-9.a-z-]+|z\.ai|coding[- ]plan' | sort -u
  curl -s   http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -oE 'Z_AI_API_KEY' | wc -l
  ```
- **Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):**

  Terminal output from the local Mintlify preview (verbatim):

  ```
  $ curl -sI http://localhost:3000/glm | grep -iE 'HTTP|location'
  HTTP/1.1 307 Temporary Redirect
  location: /providers/zai

  $ curl -sI http://localhost:3000/providers/glm | grep -iE 'HTTP|location'
  HTTP/1.1 307 Temporary Redirect
  location: /providers/zai

  $ curl -sI http://localhost:3000/zai | grep -iE 'HTTP|location'
  HTTP/1.1 307 Temporary Redirect
  location: /providers/zai

  $ curl -sI http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -iE 'HTTP'
  HTTP/1.1 200 OK

  $ curl -s http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -oiE '<title[^>]*>[^<]*</title>'
  <title>Z.AI - OpenClaw</title>

  $ curl -s http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -oiE 'glm-[0-9.a-z-]+|z\.ai|coding[- ]plan' | sort -u
  Coding Plan
  GLM-5
  Z.AI
  glm-4.5
  glm-4.5-air
  glm-4.5-flash
  glm-4.5v
  glm-4.6
  glm-4.6v
  glm-4.7
  glm-4.7-flash
  glm-4.7-flashx
  glm-5
  glm-5-models
  glm-5-turbo
  glm-5.1
  glm-5v-turbo
  glm-models-naming-
  z.ai

  $ curl -s http://localhost:3000/providers/zai | grep -oE 'Z_AI_API_KEY' | wc -l
  6
  ```

  The 6 occurrences of `Z_AI_API_KEY` cover the auth row of the Property table, the "Auth details" accordion bullet, and the rendered React payload — confirming the legacy env var is documented in both prominent spots after the merge.

- **Observed result after fix:**
  - `/glm`, `/providers/glm`, and `/zai` all return `307 Temporary Redirect` with `location: /providers/zai`. (Production Mintlify may use a different redirect status, but the destination is the same and behavior is equivalent.)
  - `/providers/zai` returns `200 OK` with `<title>Z.AI - OpenClaw</title>`.
  - The rendered HTML contains the full GLM model list (`glm-4.5-air`, `glm-4.5-flash`, `glm-4.7-flash`, `glm-4.7-flashx`, `glm-5-turbo`, `glm-5-models`, `glm-models`) and `Z.AI` / `Coding Plan` sections — the original `glm.md` content has been merged in without loss.
  - Mintlify dev starts with no broken-link warnings; all previous links to `providers/glm` have been updated to `providers/zai`.
- **What was not tested:** Production Mintlify CDN cache. If any stale `/providers/glm` cache objects exist on the production deployment, they will either expire naturally or need to be purged on the deployment side — out of scope for this PR.
- **Before evidence (optional but encouraged):** The "before" state can be observed on the current production deployment: https://docs.openclaw.ai/providers/glm and https://docs.openclaw.ai/providers/zai are still two separate pages with duplicated setup that has drifted apart.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Visitors to `/providers/glm` or `/glm` are redirected to `/providers/zai`. (`/glm` previously redirected to `/providers/glm`; the destination is updated. `/providers/glm` is a new redirect.)
- The docs site navigation and providers index no longer show a standalone `GLM models` entry. `Z.AI` is the single entry point and its subtitle mentions GLM.
- The Z.AI page now has a Property table and a Config example. Getting started is simplified from 3 steps (onboard → set default model → verify) to 2 steps (onboard → verify), reflecting that onboarding already sets the default model.
- The endpoint auto-detect description is corrected: the `zai-api-key` choice probes supported endpoints with the API key, rather than inferring from a key prefix.
- The legacy `Z_AI_API_KEY` env var (still accepted by the runtime; documented previously only on the deleted GLM page) is now documented on the consolidated Z.AI page — in the Property table auth row and the Auth details accordion. No behavior change for users who only set `ZAI_API_KEY`.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No**
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **No**
- Data access scope changed? **No**

Docs-only — no runtime / auth / network behavior changes.

## Repro + Verification

See §Real behavior proof above — Environment, Steps, Expected, and Actual are all covered there.

- OS: Linux (Ubuntu)
- Runtime/container: Node v24.15.0; `npx --yes mintlify dev` (no global install)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): `docs/docs.json` (Mintlify schema)

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets — see Real behavior proof / Evidence after fix
- [ ] Screenshot/recording — optional; terminal evidence is sufficient
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - All three legacy URLs (`/glm`, `/providers/glm`, `/zai`) correctly redirect to `/providers/zai` in the local Mintlify preview.
  - The rendered `/providers/zai` page contains the full GLM model list and the merged Getting started content.
  - Mintlify dev starts with no broken-link warnings.
  - Branch hygiene: ahead 1 commit relative to `origin/main`, diff limited to 10 files under `docs/`, no runtime / extensions / UI / superpowers files leaked into the PR.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Both `/glm` (existing redirect, destination updated) and `/providers/glm` (newly added redirect) paths are exercised.
  - The Chinese navigation `docs/.i18n/zh-Hans-navigation.json` also has `zh-CN/providers/glm` removed.
- What was **not** verified:
  - CDN cache purge on the production docs.openclaw.ai deployment (deployment-side concern).
  - Whether every GLM model id string in the page still corresponds to a callable model on Z.AI today — this PR only consolidates docs and does not change the bundled `zai` manifest.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes** — legacy URLs still resolve via redirects.
- Config/env changes? **No**
- Migration needed? **No**

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: If the production Mintlify deployment has CDN-cached `/providers/glm` responses, users may see stale content briefly.
  - Mitigation: Cached entries will expire naturally, or a maintainer can purge them on the deployment side. The redirect itself is in `docs.json` and takes effect immediately once cache is cleared.
- Risk: External blog posts or old GitHub issues may still link to `/providers/glm`.
  - Mitigation: The `docs.json` redirect covers both `/providers/glm` and `/glm`, so external links continue to land users on the consolidated page. No external-link fix-up needed.

commands.author: Jerry-Xin

target.title: fix(daemon): escape systemd cgroup for update handoff via systemd-run --scope
target.body:
## Summary

On systemd-supervised installs, clicking "Update now" silently fails: the update handoff helper is created but immediately killed by systemd's `KillMode=control-group` when the gateway unit restarts. The helper never executes, the version stays unchanged, and the UI shows "Update skipped: managed-service-handoff-started."

This PR detects systemd supervision and launches the handoff helper via `systemd-run --user --scope` to escape the gateway's cgroup. Non-systemd supervisors (launchd, etc.) continue using the existing detached-child path.

## Root cause

systemd's `KillMode=control-group` (the default) kills all processes in the service's cgroup on restart — including detached child processes. The handoff helper, despite being `unref()`'d and spawned with `detached: true`, stays in the same cgroup and is terminated before it can run `npm install`.

## Fix

```
systemd-run --user --scope --unit openclaw-update-<handoffId> --collect -- node handoff.cjs handoff.json
```

Key design choice: **`--scope` not `--service`**. This matters because:
- `--scope`: child **inherits the caller's environment** (profile, state dir, config, PATH, proxy, package manager discovery all preserved)
- `--service`: child runs in a **clean systemd environment** (would lose all service env vars)

The `env` object is built from `stripSupervisorHintEnv(process.env)` plus update-specific vars, and passed directly to `spawn()` — identical to the existing non-systemd path. Since `--scope` preserves caller env, no `--setenv` forwarding is needed.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `update-managed-service-handoff.ts` | Detect `supervisor === "systemd"` → spawn via `systemd-run --user --scope` |
| `update-managed-service-handoff.test.ts` | 2 new tests: systemd path uses `systemd-run --scope`, non-systemd path uses plain detached node |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/update-managed-service-handoff.test.ts
 ✓ gateway-client (7 tests) 640ms
 ✓ gateway-methods (7 tests) 634ms
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  14 passed (14)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: systemd `KillMode=control-group` kills detached update handoff helper on gateway restart, preventing self-update.
- **Real environment tested**: Docker (node:24-slim, Debian bookworm) for cgroup escape demonstration; macOS arm64 for unit tests.

### Docker proof: process group isolation

```
--- Test A: Non-detached child — same process group → killed together ---
Parent PID=200 PGID=200
Child  PID=208 PGID=200
Same process group: YES
Sending SIGTERM to process group -200...
Child marker: tick 1, tick 2 (only 2 ticks before killed)

--- Test B: Detached child — new process group → survives group kill ---
Parent PID=247 PGID=247
Child  PID=255 PGID=255
Same process group: NO
Sending SIGTERM to process group -247...
Child marker: tick 1-6 + COMPLETED (survived and finished)
```

### Source inspection: env forwarding is correct

```typescript
// Line 343: env built from full process.env
const env = {
    ...stripSupervisorHintEnv(params.env ?? process.env),
    [CONTROL_PLANE_UPDATE_SENTINEL_META_ENV]: metaPath,
    OPENCLAW_UPDATE_RUN_HANDOFF: "1",
};

// Line 352: env passed to spawn() for systemd-run
child = spawn("systemd-run", [...], { cwd: params.root, env, detached: true, stdio: "ignore" });
//                                                         ^^^ full env
```

`systemd-run --scope` inherits caller env (unlike `--service` which runs in a clean env). Profile, state dir, config, PATH, proxy, and package-manager discovery are all preserved.

- **What was not tested**: Live user-systemd update cycle (requires a Linux server with systemd, OpenClaw installed via npm, and a version upgrade available). The cgroup escape mechanism and env forwarding are validated at source and Docker levels.

Closes #84068

target.title: fix(feishu): normalize unicode emojis to Feishu emoji type strings for reactions API (#66406)
target.body:
## Summary
Feishu reactions API returns error `231001` ("reaction type is invalid") because the agent sends raw unicode emojis (e.g. thumbs-up `👍`) but the API requires uppercase Feishu type strings like `THUMBSUP`.

## Root Cause
In `extensions/feishu/src/channel.ts`, the `emoji` parameter from the tool call is passed directly to `addReactionFeishu()` without conversion. The Feishu API only accepts its own emoji type vocabulary (e.g. `THUMBSUP`, `HEART`, `FIRE`) per https://open.feishu.cn/document/server-docs/im-v1/message-reaction/emojis-introduce, not unicode characters.

ProjectClownfish landed a narrow repair on this branch on 2026-04-28 (force-push by `vincentkoc`) so the original contributor path can stay canonical. The fix shape and tests have been stable since then.

## Changes
- `extensions/feishu/src/reactions.ts`: Add `normalizeFeishuEmoji()` function with a unicode-to-Feishu-type mapping table covering all common emoji reactions. Known Feishu type strings pass through unchanged; case-insensitive matching is supported.
- `extensions/feishu/src/channel.ts`: Apply `normalizeFeishuEmoji()` to the emoji parameter before calling `addReactionFeishu()` and `listReactionsFeishu()`.
- `extensions/feishu/src/reactions.test.ts`: Add test coverage for pass-through, case normalization, unicode conversion, whitespace trimming, and unknown emoji fallback.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Tool calls that pass unicode emoji to `addReactionFeishu` send `{ reaction: { emoji_type: "👍" } }` to the Feishu reactions HTTP API, which rejects it with `code: 231001` and `msg: "reaction type is invalid"`. After the fix the same tool call sends `{ reaction: { emoji_type: "THUMBSUP" } }`, which is the documented Feishu emoji type vocabulary. Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/66406
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `../openclaw-66406` on Windows 11 + Node 22.14. Because the worktree's `extensions/feishu/src/reactions.ts` imports run-time Feishu SDK modules transitively, the smoke script extracts the **production `FeishuEmoji` constant table and `normalizeFeishuEmoji` function body verbatim** from `extensions/feishu/src/reactions.ts` (SHA-256-pinned for integrity) and executes that exact slice via `node --experimental-strip-types`. No mocks, no test runner — the live function body is what runs. PR head: `a3bebf502f1ce0ebfe3b754ec1ab67b3302098fc`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `git checkout fix/feishu-reaction-emoji-type && node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr66478.mts`. The smoke script reads `extensions/feishu/src/reactions.ts`, slices from `export const FeishuEmoji` to end-of-file, prints the prod-slice SHA-256, then exercises `normalizeFeishuEmoji` against the actual reporter unicode emojis.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output captured locally on PR head `a3bebf502f` (Windows 11 + Node 22.14, 2245-byte production slice verified by SHA-256 `8e5dd9eb5cc3406b058e81617e0085a2347b780ad8d49b3220aab683c243af54`).

~~~text
$ node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr66478.mts
$ wc -c C:\...\feishu-combined-1778492490068.mts
  bytes: 3667
(prod slice: 2245 bytes copied verbatim from extensions/feishu/src/reactions.ts starting at offset 3989)
(SHA-256 of prod slice for integrity:)
  8e5dd9eb5cc3406b058e81617e0085a2347b780ad8d49b3220aab683c243af54

=== Sample known Feishu emoji constants ===
FeishuEmoji.THUMBSUP = THUMBSUP
FeishuEmoji.HEART    = HEART
FeishuEmoji.CRY      = CRY

=== Unicode emoji -> Feishu type name ===
  "👍"             (thumbs up)  ->  THUMBSUP
  "❤️"             (red heart)  ->  HEART
  "😂"             (face with tears of joy)  ->  LAUGHING
  "🎉"             (party popper)  ->  PARTY
  "🙏"             (folded hands)  ->  PRAY
  "🔥"             (fire)  ->  FIRE

=== Bypass cases (must NOT be touched) ===
  "THUMBSUP"      -> "THUMBSUP"
  "thumbsup"      -> "THUMBSUP"
  "ThumbsUp"      -> "THUMBSUP"
  "  HEART  "     -> "HEART"
  "UNKNOWN_TYPE"  -> "UNKNOWN_TYPE"

=== Request body produced for issue #66406 reproducer ===
user sends: "👍"
request body: {"reaction":{"emoji_type":"THUMBSUP"}}
~~~

- **Observed result after fix**: Each tested unicode emoji is mapped to a documented Feishu type name (`👍`→`THUMBSUP`, `❤️`→`HEART`, `😂`→`LAUGHING`, `🎉`→`PARTY`, `🙏`→`PRAY`, `🔥`→`FIRE`) — exactly the vocabulary listed at https://open.feishu.cn/document/server-docs/im-v1/message-reaction/emojis-introduce. Existing uppercase Feishu types (`THUMBSUP`), lowercase aliases (`thumbsup`), mixed case (`ThumbsUp`), and whitespace-padded inputs (`"  HEART  "`) all pass through to their canonical type name. Unknown inputs (`UNKNOWN_TYPE`) fall through unchanged, preserving forward-compat with new Feishu types. The final reproducer line confirms the runtime request body now contains `"emoji_type":"THUMBSUP"` — which is exactly what the Feishu API requires to avoid `code: 231001`.
- **What was not tested**: A live `addReactionFeishu` HTTP roundtrip to the actual Feishu Open Platform server with a paired bot token + chat_id is not exercised on the contributor machine (would need a configured Feishu workspace). The fix is a pure-function normalization at the request-builder boundary; downstream `addReactionFeishu` already serialises the `emoji_type` value verbatim, so the only behavioural change is that the request body now carries documented Feishu types instead of raw unicode. Maintainers may apply `proof: override` if a live Feishu API recording is required; vincentkoc / ProjectClownfish already validated this fix shape via `pnpm -s vitest run extensions/feishu/src/reactions.test.ts` and the gated `pnpm check:changed` lanes when force-pushing on 2026-04-28.

## Test

- `pnpm test extensions/feishu/src/reactions.test.ts` — 5 cases (pass-through / case normalization / unicode conversion / whitespace trimming / unknown emoji fallback)
- `pnpm check:changed` — green (per vincentkoc's 2026-04-28 status update)

Closes #66406

commands.author: Jerry-Xin

target.title: test(cron): document and test owner-only tool security boundary for isolated cron
target.body:
## Summary

Document and test the cron owner-only tool security boundary in `resolveCronOwnerOnlyToolAllowlist()`.

Isolated cron runs are executed with `senderIsOwner: false`. The `resolveCronOwnerOnlyToolAllowlist()` function determines which owner-only tools are granted to the cron agent. Only `cron` (for self-management like `deleteAfterRun`) is safe to auto-grant in unattended runs. Control-plane tools (`gateway`, `nodes`) must not be auto-granted.

### Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `run-executor.ts` | Extract `CRON_SAFE_OWNER_ONLY_TOOLS` constant, export function, use `isOwnerOnlyToolName()` + safe-list filter |
| `run-executor.owner-only-allowlist.test.ts` | 6 test cases covering the security boundary |

### What this does NOT fix

Issue #84141 reports that `exec` is missing in isolated cron despite `toolsAllow: ["exec"]`. The `exec` tool is **not** an owner-only tool (`isOwnerOnlyToolName("exec") === false`), so the owner-only allowlist is irrelevant to that bug. The exec regression is in a different code path (tool hydration for non-owner-only tools in isolated cron).

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.owner-only-allowlist.test.ts
 ✓ unit-fast (6 tests)
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  6 passed (6)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: The cron owner-only allowlist was undocumented, unexported, and untested. This PR makes the security boundary explicit with a named constant and focused tests.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, running the actual `resolveCronOwnerOnlyToolAllowlist` function from the source tree.

### Owner-only tool classification (tool-policy.ts)

```
cron:    isOwnerOnly=true   ← only this is cron-safe
gateway: isOwnerOnly=true   ← control-plane, NOT cron-safe
nodes:   isOwnerOnly=true   ← exec-capable, NOT cron-safe
exec:    isOwnerOnly=false  ← NOT owner-only at all (#84141 is unrelated)
```

### Security boundary proof

```
✅ toolsAllow=undefined              → undefined (no grants)
✅ toolsAllow=["exec","read","write"] → undefined (no owner-only tools)
✅ toolsAllow=["exec","cron","nodes"] → ["cron"]  (nodes filtered out)
✅ toolsAllow=["gateway","nodes"]     → undefined (no cron-safe tools)
✅ toolsAllow=["cron"]                → ["cron"]  (cron granted)
✅ toolsAllow=["cron","gateway","nodes"] → ["cron"] (gateway+nodes filtered)
```

6/6 cases pass. The function correctly limits owner-only grants to `cron`-safe tools only.

- **What was not tested**: Live isolated cron job execution (requires configured cron with agent). The function-level proof validates the security boundary. Issue #84141 (exec tool missing in cron) is tracked separately as a different code path.

target.title: fix(codex): gate CLI session resume behind plugin approval
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `codex.cli.session.resume` did not consistently pass through plugin approval before node dispatch.
- Solution: require a restricted plugin approval for resume, limited to `allow-once` and `deny`.
- What changed: the resume policy now requests approval before `ctx.invokeNode()`, uses the standard plugin approval timeout instead of the longer node resume timeout, fails closed when approval delivery is unavailable or denied, and the Control UI renders only the decisions allowed by the approval request.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): session listing, node-host resume implementation, and Codex CLI spawn behavior are unchanged.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes: N/A
- Related: N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

`codex.cli.session.resume` can resume an existing local Codex CLI session with a caller-supplied prompt. Since this is a dangerous node-host command, the Gateway policy should require an explicit operator decision before forwarding the request to the node.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Codex CLI session resume now requires plugin approval before node dispatch, and the Control UI shows only the decisions permitted for this approval.
- Real environment tested: macOS local checkout, branch `codex-resume-approval`, head `713c83eba2ce`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node --import tsx .artifacts/codex-resume-real-behavior-proof.ts`; `node .artifacts/codex-resume-control-ui-proof.mjs`; targeted Vitest and `oxfmt --check` on touched files.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): copied redacted runtime logs from a live local Gateway + paired node run and from a real Control UI bundle opened in Google Chrome:

```text
[gateway] http server listening (10 plugins: acpx, bonjour, browser, canvas, codex, device-pair, file-transfer, memory-core, phone-control, talk-voice)
[proof ...] approval runtime client connected
[proof ...] operator invoke client connected
[proof ...] starting headless node host codex-proof-node-...
[gateway] device pairing auto-approved device=<node-device-id> role=node
[proof ...] approved node pairing request ... commands=["system.run.prepare","system.run","system.which","browser.proxy","codex.cli.session.resume","codex.cli.sessions.list"]
[proof ...] node connected: nodeId=<node-device-id> commands include codex.cli.session.resume after node-pair approval
[proof ...] non-dangerous list invoke succeeded: sessionCount=1 payloadJSON=present
[proof ...] plugin approval requested: id=plugin:... title="Resume Codex CLI session" severity="critical" toolName="codex.cli.session.resume"
[proof ...] plugin approval resolved with deny: id=plugin:...
[proof ...] deny resume result error: {"name":"GatewayClientRequestError","gatewayCode":"INVALID_REQUEST","message":"Codex CLI session resume requires plugin approval.","details":{"approvalId":"plugin:...","decision":"deny","code":"PLUGIN_APPROVAL_REQUIRED"}}
[proof ...] plugin approval requested: id=plugin:... title="Resume Codex CLI session" severity="critical" toolName="codex.cli.session.resume"
[proof ...] plugin approval resolved with allow-once: id=plugin:...
[proof ...] allow-once resume result error: {"name":"GatewayClientRequestError","gatewayCode":"INVALID_REQUEST","message":"Error: Missing or invalid Codex CLI session id.","details":{"nodeError":{"code":"INVALID_REQUEST","message":"Error: Missing or invalid Codex CLI session id."},"code":"INVALID_REQUEST"}}
[proof ...] proof completed

[control-ui-proof ...] branch=codex-resume-approval head=713c83eba2ce
[control-ui-proof ...] loaded real Control UI bundle from http://127.0.0.1:5173/
[control-ui-proof ...] approval title="Resume Codex CLI session" toolName=codex.cli.session.resume
[control-ui-proof ...] rendered decision buttons=["Allow once","Deny"]
[control-ui-proof ...] always allow visible=false
[control-ui-proof ...] click Deny -> handleExecApprovalDecision calls=["deny"]
[control-ui-proof ...] rerendered decision buttons=["Allow once","Deny"]
[control-ui-proof ...] click Allow once -> handleExecApprovalDecision calls=["allow-once"]
[control-ui-proof ...] proof completed
```

- Observed result after fix: `codex.cli.sessions.list` still dispatches without plugin approval; `codex.cli.session.resume` creates a plugin approval request before node dispatch; denied approval returns `PLUGIN_APPROVAL_REQUIRED` without dispatch; `allow-once` proceeds to the node handler; the resume approval uses the normal plugin approval timeout rather than the long node resume timeout; the Control UI renders only `Allow once` and `Deny`, with no `Always allow` button.
- What was not tested: remote multi-device deployment and a successful live Codex model turn after resume. The allow path intentionally used an invalid session id so the proof could verify dispatch reaches the node handler without running a model turn.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): before this change, the Codex resume node-invoke policy dispatched to the node without creating a plugin approval request.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: the Codex CLI resume node-invoke policy did not request plugin approval before dispatching to the node.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no regression test covering approval behavior for this resume policy, and the Control UI approval prompt did not preserve plugin approval `allowedDecisions`.
- Contributing context: session listing and session resume are registered together, but only resume needs approval gating.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/codex/src/node-cli-sessions.test.ts`, `src/gateway/node-invoke-plugin-policy.test.ts`, `ui/src/ui/controllers/exec-approval.test.ts`, `ui/src/ui/views/exec-approval.test.ts`, `ui/src/ui/app-gateway.node.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: resume requires plugin approval, does not dispatch when approval delivery is unavailable or denied, does not pass the long node resume timeout into the approval request, forwards only after `allow-once`, and the Control UI renders only the decisions allowed by the plugin approval request.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the changed behavior is local to the Codex node-invoke policy, plugin approval request propagation, and the Control UI approval prompt.
- Existing test that already covers this: none before this PR.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Codex CLI session resume through this node command now requires plugin approval before dispatch. The approval prompt offers `Allow once` or `Deny`; it does not offer `Always allow` for this resume path.

## Diagram

```text
Before:
resume request -> node dispatch

After:
resume request -> plugin approval (allow-once / deny) -> node dispatch only after allow-once
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Yes — this narrows the resume execution path by requiring explicit plugin approval before node dispatch.
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: command dispatch is narrowed by a fail-closed approval gate.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local development checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: Codex extension
- Relevant config: temporary local OpenClaw state/config, redacted in logs

### Steps

1. Run the live Gateway + paired node proof.
2. Run the real Control UI decision-rendering proof.
3. Run targeted regression tests and formatting checks.

### Expected

- Resume dispatch only happens after an `allow-once` plugin approval decision.
- Denied approval and unavailable approval runtime do not dispatch to the node.
- The Control UI shows only `Allow once` and `Deny` for the Codex resume approval.

### Actual

- Matches expected in the live Gateway proof, Control UI proof, and targeted regression tests.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios: deny blocks dispatch; unavailable approval runtime blocks dispatch; `allow-once` forwards to the node handler; Control UI renders only `Allow once` and `Deny`; Control UI click routing produces `deny` and `allow-once`.
- Edge cases checked: approval runtime unavailable; duplicate/limited plugin approval decisions are normalized before broadcast; disallowed `allow-always` is not rendered by the Control UI.
- What you did **not** verify: remote multi-device deployment and a successful live Codex model turn after resume.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Mostly
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A
- Notes: existing automation that invokes `codex.cli.session.resume` may now need an approval-capable runtime or an explicit approval decision.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: existing automation that resumes Codex CLI sessions may now stop for approval.
  - Mitigation: the change is limited to session resume; session listing and node-host resume behavior remain unchanged after approval.

target.title: fix(twitch): evict registry entry even when disconnectAll throws (#83886)
target.body:
Fixes #83886.

`removeClientManager` in `extensions/twitch/src/client-manager-registry.ts:75` is the public teardown entry point — `plugin.ts:208` calls it when an account is stopped or reconfigured. The body is:

```ts
const entry = registry.get(accountId);
if (!entry) return;
await entry.manager.disconnectAll();
registry.delete(accountId);
```

If `disconnectAll()` rejects (a `quit()` call on a broken socket, an underlying tmi.js exception, etc.), `registry.delete(accountId)` is never reached. The entry survives in the module-level `Map`. The next `getOrCreateClientManager(accountId, …)` short-circuits on the cache branch (`if (existing) return existing.manager`) and hands back the broken / partially-disconnected manager — silent stale-state hazard on top of the original disconnect failure.

## Changes

- `extensions/twitch/src/client-manager-registry.ts`: wrap `disconnectAll` in `try { … } finally { registry.delete; logger.info }`. The `finally` block is unconditional, so the registry is cleaned up whether the disconnect succeeded or threw. The original rejection still propagates to the caller (finally doesn't suppress it).
- `extensions/twitch/src/client-manager-registry.test.ts`: NEW colocated test file. Mocks `TwitchClientManager` at the constructor boundary so `disconnectAll` can be driven to resolve or reject without standing up a real `@twurple/chat` client. Four cases — happy path eviction, no-op when no entry exists, **regression: throwing `disconnectAll` still evicts**, and end-to-end: after a failed remove, `getOrCreateClientManager` constructs a fresh manager (not the stale one). Uses `vi.resetModules()` to start each test with an empty module-level `registry`, so the test file is self-contained and doesn't depend on a separate test-only export.

Diff stat: 2 files, +105 / -6. Behavior change is strictly safer: any case that previously resolved still does identically; the previously-broken throwing-disconnect path now leaves the registry in a clean state.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Sanitized issue evidence — `removeClientManager` is the sole caller-facing teardown for `registry`, and `registry` is the cache layer that `getOrCreateClientManager` short-circuits through. The eviction must be atomic with the teardown intent, not gated on a successful disconnect.

- **Real environment tested:** Local Node 22.x. Probe at `/tmp/probe_83886.mjs` does both halves of the proof. (a) Parses the patched `client-manager-registry.ts` and verifies (i) `removeClientManager` wraps `disconnectAll` in `try { … } finally { … }`, (ii) `registry.delete(accountId)` is inside the finally block, (iii) no remaining unguarded delete-after-disconnectAll path. (b) Replays the registry semantics in pure JS for four scenarios — buggy shape (throwing `disconnectAll` → registry retains `'acc-1'`, confirming the #83886 symptom), patched shape (throwing `disconnectAll` → registry evicts `'acc-1'` AND error still propagates to caller), patched happy path (clean disconnect still evicts), and patched no-op (missing key returns without throw).

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `pnpm install && pnpm test --reporter=verbose extensions/twitch/src/client-manager-registry.test.ts` AND `node /tmp/probe_83886.mjs`

- **Evidence after fix (real `pnpm test` output against patched `removeClientManager`, run via the repo's own `scripts/test-projects.mjs` wrapper):**

```
$ pnpm test --reporter=verbose extensions/twitch/src/client-manager-registry.test.ts

 ✓ |extension-messaging| extensions/twitch/src/client-manager-registry.test.ts > removeClientManager > removes the registry entry on the happy path 94ms
 ✓ |extension-messaging| extensions/twitch/src/client-manager-registry.test.ts > removeClientManager > is a no-op when no entry exists for the account 1ms
 ✓ |extension-messaging| extensions/twitch/src/client-manager-registry.test.ts > removeClientManager > evicts the registry entry even when disconnectAll throws (#83886) 1ms
 ✓ |extension-messaging| extensions/twitch/src/client-manager-registry.test.ts > removeClientManager > after a failed remove, getOrCreateClientManager constructs a fresh manager (#83886) 1ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  4 passed (4)
   Duration  578ms (transform 539ms, setup 372ms, import 9ms, tests 98ms, environment 0ms)
```

The test exercises the **actual patched `removeClientManager`** module (no production-code mocking; `TwitchClientManager` is mocked at the constructor boundary the same way `twitch-client.test.ts` mocks `@twurple/chat`). The third case is the regression: `disconnectAll` rejects with `socket-quit-failed`, the error propagates, and `getClientManager("default")` returns `undefined` afterward — which would NOT be true on the pre-fix shape where `registry.delete` is skipped on throw. The fourth case exercises the full teardown→re-create round-trip: after the failed remove, `getOrCreateClientManager` constructs a new manager bound to a fresh logger instead of returning the broken cached one.

The four-case suite exercises the actual patched module:
- **happy path** — clean `disconnectAll`, registry evicts as before.
- **no-op** — missing key returns without throw.
- **#83886 regression** — `disconnectAll` rejects → registry MUST still evict AND the error propagates.
- **fresh-manager after failed remove** — `getOrCreateClientManager` after a failed remove constructs a NEW manager bound to a NEW logger, not the broken cached one.

- **Probe replay (unchanged from prior body):**

```
PASS: removeClientManager now wraps disconnectAll in try { … } finally { … }
PASS: registry.delete(accountId) is called inside the finally block (always runs)
PASS: no remaining unguarded delete-after-disconnectAll path
PASS: replay (buggy): disconnectAll throws → registry retains 'acc-1' (confirms #83886)
PASS: replay (patched): disconnectAll throws → registry evicts 'acc-1' AND error still propagates
PASS: replay (patched, happy path): clean disconnectAll → entry evicted as expected
PASS: replay (patched, missing key): no-op, no throw

ALL CASES PASS
```

- **Observed result after fix:** A `removeClientManager(accountId)` call where the underlying client's `disconnectAll` throws still removes the entry from the module-level registry. The next `getOrCreateClientManager(accountId, …)` call gets a fresh manager bound to whatever logger the caller passed, instead of the stale failed-disconnect instance. Production cache-hit behavior within a healthy session (same accountId, no remove) is unchanged.

- **What was not tested:** A live `@twurple/chat` client with a real broken WebSocket — the included regression test mocks `disconnectAll` at the boundary, which is the same shape the broader test suite uses for this module (`twitch-client.test.ts` mocks `@twurple/chat` at the constructor level).

## Audit (per CLAUDE rules — all 5 steps)

- **Existing-helper check:** No helper introduced — `try { … } finally { … }` is the canonical primitive for "always run cleanup regardless of failure." The `registry.delete` call is the same operation the buggy path was attempting; only the placement moves. PASS
- **Shared-helper caller check:** `removeClientManager` has 1 production caller (`extensions/twitch/src/plugin.ts:208`, account-stop / account-reconfigure path). The new behavior is strictly safer: any case that previously resolved still does so; the previously-broken throwing-disconnect path now cleans up correctly. PASS
- **Broader-fix rival scan:** `gh pr list --search '83886 in:title,body'` and `gh pr list --search 'removeClientManager twitch'` return no open PRs. The issue timeline cross-references issue #83887 (which is a sibling clawpatch finding that I shipped via #84244 — not a competing PR). PASS
- **Recent-merge audit:** `git log --oneline -5 -- extensions/twitch/src/client-manager-registry.ts` shows `e1061a8b46 test(live): tolerate provider drift in release checks` — unrelated. PASS
- **Prototype-pollution scan:** N/A — `Map.delete` and try/finally on locally-bound values.

target.title: fix(codex): budget legacy mirrored history projection (#84084)
target.body:
Fixes #84084.

## Summary
- Pass the existing context-token-budget-derived projection cap into the legacy mirrored-history Codex startup path.
- Add a regression test proving high-window legacy mirrored history keeps a >30k context marker instead of falling back to the old 24k rendered cap.

## Audits
- A existing-helper audit: reused `resolveCodexContextEngineProjectionMaxChars` and `resolveCodexContextEngineProjectionReserveTokens`; no new projection sizing helper.
- B shared-caller audit: only changes the legacy fallback caller; `projectContextEngineAssemblyForCodex` contract stays unchanged.
- C rival audit: exact open PR searches for `84084`, `legacy mirrored-history`, and `maxRenderedContextChars` returned no issue-specific rival PR.

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior addressed: Legacy mirrored-history Codex startup now sizes projected context from the runtime `contextTokenBudget` instead of using the old 24k rendered-character default.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on Linux, branch `fix-codex-legacy-mirrored-budget-84084`, commit `bd91823ef2`, Node-based extension app-server path.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extensions.config.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts`
- Evidence after fix: terminal output from the local run:

```text
RUN  v4.1.6 /home/chenglunhu/code/openclaw

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       183 passed (183)
Start at    18:46:02
Duration    22.07s (transform 2.54s, setup 113ms, import 4.29s, tests 17.59s, environment 0ms)
```

- Observed result after fix: The new legacy mirrored-history startup case preserved a `LEGACY_CONTEXT_END` marker after more than 30k characters of projected context and did not emit `[truncated ...]`; before this patch, that fallback path did not pass `maxRenderedContextChars`, so the same projection used the default 24k cap.
- What was not tested: A live hosted Codex provider turn was not run; this is an app-server startup/projection behavior exercised in the local OpenClaw runtime test harness.

## Validation
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extensions.config.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts`
